The Human Flag is an impressive and widly known skill. It is usually done on a pole or wall bars. Hereby the body is hold parallel to the ground, only supported by the arms. Like with all unilateral exercises, always work on both sides.
Execution: Your lower arm presses into the anchor point with elevated shoulder blades. At the same time your upper arm helps you balance your tightened body, by pulling with your lats and depressing your shoulder blade. Keep both arms straight. Engage your obliques to rotate your straight legs sideways. The end point is reached, when your legs are parallel to the ground. Keep a straight body and open up your chest, which should be perpendicular to the floor. The most important muscles in this skill are your shoulders, because there will be the highest force of gravity. To avoid disbalances you should always train both sides
list.techniqueSummary:
* Straight arms
* Pulling arm with depressed scapula and pronated grip
* Pushing arm with elevated scapula and supinated grip
* Legs and body parallel to the ground
* Chest open

list.name: Incline Push Up
list.description:
The Incline Push Up is an easier variation to the standard Push Up.

Execution: Find something where it is save to support yourself in an 45° (or less) angle. This could be a table, a chair, a lower bar or anything which suits the need. Start in a plank position with your hands on the support. Your arms should be a bit more than shoulder wide apart. Your hands should be in line with your shoulders. Press your shoulders down (proctracted) and back (depressed). Turn your pelves in and keep a good tension in your whole body. Slowly lower yourself down and don't allow your elbows to turn widely outwards. Before your chest almost touches the support, feel how your shoulder blades squeeze together and push yourself back up.

Variations: The difficulty of the exercise can be adjust from vertical Wall Push Ups, to horizontal normal Push Ups. The more vertical you go, the easier it is.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Keep elbows in
* Scapula depressed
* Till pelves backward
* Tight body
* Full range of motion (ROM)
* Don't arch back

list.name: Plank
list.description:
The Plank is a basic exercise to work on your stability endurance.

Execution: Bend your arms to 90° and out your forearms to the ground. Your legs should be straight and together. Take on a horizontal and straight body position. Your pelvic should be normal or tilt backwards. This is very important, as you can cheat easily by bringing arching your back or bringing your glute to high. Furthermore your shoulder blades should be depressed and protacted. Keep your core engaged and stable. Hold that position.

Variations: There are many variations of the Plank. E.g. you can lift one arm and the diagonal leg to increase the difficulty. You can also put your arms further away in front of you and even extend them. To decrease the difficulty you can perform the Plank on your knees.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Scapula depressed and protracted
* Till pelves backward
* Tight horizontal body
* Don't arch back

list.name: Diamond Push Up
list.description:
The Diamond Push Up is a variation of the normal Push Up, which will focus more on your triceps.

Execution: Form a triangle with your hands and place them on the ground in front of your chest. Your legs should be straight and together. Take on a horizontal and straight body position. Your pelvic should be normal or tilt backwards. This is very important, as you can cheat easily by bringing arching your back or bringing your glute to high. Furthermore your shoulder blades should be depressed and protacted. Keep your core engaged and stable. Initiate the movement by bending your arms and bringing your chest towards the ground. When your chest touches your hands, push yourself up into the starting position.

Variations: To increase the difficulty you can raise one leg into the air (Scorpion Push Up). Furthermore the exercise gets much harder if your hands are above your belly instead your chest. This will challenge your shoulder strength a lot.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Scapula depressed and protracted
* Till pelves backward
* Tight horizontal body
* Don't arch back

list.name: Alligator Push Up
list.description:
The Alligator Push Up (also known by Spiderman Push Up or Gecko Push Up) is a good progression, as it is an unilateral movement. Beside your strength it will also challenge your coordination and mobility. Like with all unilateral exercises, always work on both sides.

Execution: Start in a Push Up position. Start the forward movement by bringing one arm further in front of you and at the same time bend tuck your opposite leg and bring it towards your elbow. While doing so lower yourself in the bottom position of a Push Up and put your feet on the ground. Push yourself up and keep your body parallel to the ground, as your shoulder blades depressed. Now bring your other hand and leg forward and repeat the pattern.

Variations: During this exercise you will walk forwad, but you can also perform it on the same spot and increase the explosivness of the exercise. Therefore push yourself up in the air during the push up movement and switch arms and legs. This means, if your left arm was in front during the push, you should land with your right arm in front. Same for the legs.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Scapula depressed and protracted
* Knee touches elbow
* Chest parallel to the ground
* Don't arch back

list.name: One Arm Plank
list.description:
The One Arm Plank is little bit different than the normal Plank. Main goal is to prevent your core from rotating. As prepartion for the One Arm Push Up, we will also do it with a straight arm. Like with all unilateral exercises, always work on both sides.

Execution: Start in a Push Up position. Put one Arm to the side of your body and hold it there. Keep the stability and don't rotate your chest. Keep it parallel to the ground and maintain a good position. This means: Scapula depressed and protracted. Normal to posterior pelvic tilt. Straight body line, without arching or lifting the hips up. Hold the position and stabilize your body.

Variations: If you want to increase the difficulty, you can lif your diagonal opposite leg up. Furthermore, the easiest form of the exercise is if your hand is directly under your chest. You can play with different hand positioning. E.g. hand below belly in front of the head.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Scapula depressed and protracted
* Till pelves backward
* Body tight with chest parallel to ground
* Don't arch back

list.name: Incline One Arm Push Up
list.description:
The Incline One Arm Push Up is a good progression to get a feeling for the One Arm Push Up. You will have to stabilize your body from rotating, but are able to test the movement pattern with a much less leverage. Like with all unilateral exercises, always work on both sides.

Execution: Find something where it is save to support yourself in an about 45° angle. This could be a table, a chair, a lower bar or anything which suits the need. Put your hands on the support and start in a Push Up position with feets wide apart. Put one hand on your back or to your leg. Keep a straight body with chest parallel to the ground during the whole movement. Don't arch your back or rotate your body. Lower yourself in control and keep your shoulder blade depressed. Go for full range of motion until your chest almost touches your hand and push yourself up.

Variations: The difficulty of the exercise can be adjust from vertical, to horizontal position. The more vertical you go, the easier it is. The feet position can also adjust the difficulty. The wider apart, the easier. The closer your feet are together, the more you will have to counterbalance your body from rotating.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Scapula depressed and protracted
* Till pelves backward
* Body tight with chest parallel to ground
* Don't arch back

list.name: Neg. One Arm Push Up
list.description:
Excentric movements are always a nice progression towards a skill. With the Negative One Arm Push Up, you can overload your muscles and build the necessary strength for the One Arm Push Up. Like with all unilateral exercises, always work on both sides.

Execution: Start in a Push Up position with your feet wide apart. Put one hand on your back or to your leg. Keep a straight body with chest parallel to the ground during the whole movement. Don't arch your back or rotate your body. Lower yourself in control and keep your shoulder blade depressed. Keep it safe and catch yourself with your second arm in time. Don't risk falling on your face. With both arms push yourself up again. Repeat.

Variations: The slower your repetition, the harder it is. The closer your feet are together, the harder it gets.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Scapula depressed and protracted
* Till pelves backward
* Body tight with chest parallel to ground
* Don't arch back

list.name: Rocky Push Up
list.description:
This variation to the One Arm Push is called Rocky Push Up. It is inspired from the legendary training scene in the movie Rocky. It is a good progression towards the One Arm Push Up, but you should be aware that the we form is compromised only as an intermediate step towards a clean form. Like with all unilateral exercises, always work on both sides.

Execution: Start in a Push Up position with your feet really wide apart. Put one hand on the back of your leg. You can use it as support. Only for this progression it is allowed to rotate the body a bit during movement and let your elbow go out a bit. Still don't arch your back and lower yourself in control with your shoulder blade depressed. Push yourself up again. It is very important to clean up the form after progressing.

Variations: The wider your feet are apart the easier it gets, because it will decrease the leverage. You can also reduce the necessary strength by rotating your body outwards. For the clean One Arm Push Up, your body shouldn't rotate outwards, but it should be parallel to the ground. Also your feet should be as close together as you can stabilize yourself without rotating.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Feet wide apart
* Scapula depressed and protracted
* Till pelves backward
* Body is allowed to rotate a bit
* Elbow is allowed to go out a bit
* Don't arch back

list.name: One Arm Push Up
list.description:
The One Arm Push Up is a great demonstration of pushing power. Performed with clean form it can be really challenging. You see the difference with the Rocky Push Ups. Bringing your feet closer together, your elbow in and your body always straight is much harder. Like with all unilateral exercises, always work on both sides.

Execution: Start in a Push Up position with your feet shoulder wide apart or a bit wider. Only if you can keep good form, try to bring them even closer together. Put one hand on your back or leg. Your body should be straight, with chest parallel to the ground. Don't allow it to rotate during the movement, especially pushing upwards. Keep your elbow in.  Don't arch your back and lower yourself in control with your shoulder blade depressed. Push yourself up again. 

Variations: The wider your feet are apart the easier it gets, because it will decrease the leverage. You can also reduce the necessary strength by rotating your body outwards. For the clean One Arm Push Up, your body shouldn't rotate outwards, but it should be parallel to the ground. Also your feet should be as close together as you can stabilize yourself without rotating.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Feet shoulder wide apart or a bit wider
* Scapula depressed and protracted
* Till pelves backward
* Body tight with chest parallel to ground
* Keep elbow in
* Don't arch back

list.name: Downward Dog
list.description:
The Downward Dog exercise is known from Yoga. The focus seems to be on stretching, but still it is a good starting point towards your handstand journey, because it brings benefit to several points. 1. it will help you strech your shoulders. The mobility to open your shoulders is very important for a good handstand. 2. it will get you comfortable on your hands. You will get a feeling for shifting weight on your hands without much load on your wrists. 3. done correctly it will help to mimic some relevant shoulder positioning as in a handstand. Your shoulders should be rotated outward and elevated, which means that the hands and shoulders press into the ground.

Execution: Start in a an upper Push Up position. Move your hip back and up towards the sky, by pressing with your hands towards the floor and engaging your core. Your body should be in a V-shape position. Rotate your shoulder outwards, don't let them rotate inwards. Press your chest towards the ground. Keep your legs as straight as possible and if you can bring your heels towards the ground. If you are not flexible enough to straighten your legs, just keep them slightly bend.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Straight arms
* Body in V-Shape
* Shoulder rotate outwards
* Press chest towards floor

list.name: Pike Hold
list.description:
The Pike Hold will give you the opportunity to safely experiment your ability and strength to hold yourself upwards down.

Execution: First you will need something stable where you can put your feet on. This could be a box, chair, bench, ... You can start with a low elevation and work yourself up towards higher elevations. In doubt, a regular stable chair should do it. Kneel in front of the chair and put your hands on the floor. Climb your feet on top of the chair, while supporting yourself with your arms. You can keep your legs bend for the entry. If you can handle the weight on your hands, straighten your legs and your arms. The most important part for proper form is to have a straight upper body line. This means your hands, shoulders and hip should be right above each other. In order to get into this position you will need to open your shoulders and rotate them outwards. Press your arms towards the ground, which will get you a nice elevates scapula position. The hardest part will be to bring your hips into the staright line. Don't overestimate yourself by bringing the hip to far out, where you can't hold it anymore. Don't arch your back. Keep your core engaged in to the keep a posterior pelvic tilt. If you bring your head in, it will be easier to avoid an arched back and to open your shoulder. Try to look at your feet.

Variations: You can decrease or increase the difficulty of the exercise by choosing a lower or higher elevation. It is also possible to put your knees on the elevation instead of your legs. This will make the exercise easier.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Straight upper body line
* Hip over shoulder
* Shoulder over hand
* Straight arms
* Shoulder rotate outwards
* Shoulder press towards floor
* Posterior pelvic tilt

list.name: Adv. Wall Handstand
list.description:
The Advanced Wall Handstand adds the balancing to the regular Wall Handstand. Goal is to touch the wall only if necessary to correct, so you can improve your balancing.

Execution: There different entry strategies. You can just walk up the wall, with your front facing the wall. Keep your core engaged and don't arch your back. Keep your arms straight the whole time and push from your shoulders. The nearer your hands get towards the wall, the harder it gets. Also you will be challenged to keep your body straight. With an arched back you won't be able to come as close to the wall as with a hollow body position. So, if you walked up into a Wall Handstand and got comfortable, try to balance yourself without touching the wall. With your feet you can slightly push yourself away from the wall. Don't push too much or you will fall over. Also always keep it safe and have enough strength to exit safely. Especially if you are practicing the Wall Handstand with your front facing the wall. The main goal is to get comfortable balancing in the handstand. Also keep a proper form. Straight body, arms and legs. Press your shoulder into the floor, keep your core engaged and your legs squeezed together.

Variations: To avoid an arched back handstand it is recommended to perform the Wall Handstand with your front facing the wall. It will be more challenging than just kickiing up wit your back facing the wall. Also you need to take care, to always be able to exit safely. This is a bit harder when your front is facing the wall, as you will need to have some strength left to walk down the wall, or if you are able roll out.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Body in straight line and hollow position
* Shoulders open, elevated and protracted
* Pointed toes
* Straight arms
* Whole body in tension

list.name: Diamond Handstand
list.description:
The Diamond Handstand will be more challenging than the normal handstand and requires more balancing skill.

Execution: Place your hands on the ground and form a triangle with your index fingers and thumbs. Kick Up into a handstand. Don't arch your back and keep your body straight and hollow. Keep your arms straight the whole time and push from your shoulders.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Body in straight line and hollow position
* Shoulders open, elevated and protracted
* Pointed toes
* Straight arms
* Whole body in tension

list.name: Hollow Body Hold
list.description:
The Hollow Body Hold is a good exercise to teach the body alignment in a handstad and to correct arched back Handstand (Banana Handstand).

Execution: Lay with your back on the ground. Straighten your legs and arms out. Without core engagement, the will be a whole between yourh lower back and the floor. Squeeze your legs together, engage your core and rotate your hips backwards (posterior pelvic tilt). Really try to minimize or remove the whole beetwen your back and the floor. It gets easier the higher your raise your arms and legs. Try to keep your arms and legs as low as possible, without creating a whole between the floor and your back.
list.techniqueSummary:
* No whole between back and floor
* Posterior Pelvic Tilt
* Core engaged
* Legs squeezed together

list.name: Hollow Arch Roll
list.description:
The Hollow Arch Roll is a combination of the Hollow Body Hold and Superman exercises. If you are comfortable in a Hollow Body position, the Hollow Arch Roll exercise will increase the difficulty and be much more challenging to work on your core. Also it will tarket your back muscles, when your front is facing the floor.

Execution: Lay with your back on the ground. Straighten your legs and arms out. Without core engagement, the will be a whole between yourh lower back and the floor. Squeeze your legs together, engage your core and rotate your hips backwards (posterior pelvic tilt). Really try to minimize or remove the whole beetwen your back and the floor. It gets easier the higher your raise your arms and legs. Try to keep your arms and legs as low as possible, without creating a whole between the floor and your back. Hold that position for about 2-3 seconds. Roll over to your stomach on one side. While doing so, keep your arms and legs straight and try avoid that they touch the floor. If you are laying on your stomach, pull your arms and legs away from the floor and engage your back. Hold that position for about 2-3 seconds. Roll back on your backside, still trying the arms and legs to touch the floor. Hold the Hollow Body position and repeat the rotation, but this time to the other side.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Hands and feet don' touch floor
* Roll to both sides

list.name: Wall Walks
list.description:
Wall Walks can help you to increase your straight arm strength and build necessary shoulder muscles for the Handstand.

Execution: Kneel with your back in front of a wall. Put your hands on the ground and straighten your arms. Slowly and in control walk the wall up with your feets. Keep your arms straight and engage your core, so your back doesn't arch. This is really important as else there could be to much stress on your back. It is optimal if your hands are just a few centimeters away from the wall and your body is in a complete straight line from hand to toe. A simple test is, if you bring your head in, and your nose touches the wall. Still play it safe and don't come to close to the wall if you have no control or strength left.

Variations: The difficulty can be increased if you start in Push Up position. Walk up with small steps while always keeping that Hollow Body position. This will require much more endurance, as if you just walk up with 2/3 big steps.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Body in straight line and hollow position
* Shoulders open, elevated and protracted
* Pointed toes
* Straight arms
* Whole body in tension

list.name: Wall Bend Straddle Handstand
list.description:
The Wall Bend Straddle Handstand will give you a safe way to shift your weight to one hand during a Straddle Handstand. The Wall will help you to get a feeling for different body positions before you try to balane it freely.

Execution: Walk up into a Wall Handstand. Straddle your legs and shift your weight towards one arm. Therefore bend to one side and drop that leg a bit downwards. You will have to push harder into the ground with this hand and shoulder, so you can keep your balance. Keep proper Handstand form. This means Straight arms with elevated shoulders. Posterior Pelvic position. See much weight you can shift to one side safely and in control. Hold that position. Than perform the same on the other side.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Body in straight line and hollow position
* Shoulders open, elevated and protracted
* Straight arms
* Whole body in tension

list.name: Bend Straddle Handstand
list.description:
The Bend Straddle Handstand will give you a great way to shift your weight to one hand during a Straddle Handstand.

Execution: Go into a Handstand and straddle your legs. Shift your weight towards one arm. Therefore bend to one side and drop that leg a bit downwards. You will have to push harder into the ground with this hand and shoulder, so you can keep your balance. Keep proper Handstand form. This means Straight arms with elevated shoulders. Posterior Pelvic position. See much weight you can shift to one side safely and in control. Hold that position. Than perform the same on the other side.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Body in straight line and hollow position
* Shoulders open, elevated and protracted
* Straight arms
* Whole body in tension

list.name: Fingertip Straddle Handstand
list.description:
The Fingertip Straddle Handstand will force you to shift the most weight to one arm, while still getting some support from the other hand which touches the floor with the fingertips.

Execution: Walk up into a Wall Handstand. Straddle your legs and shift your weight towards one arm. Therefore bend to one side and drop that leg a bit downwards. You will have to push harder into the ground with this hand and shoulder, so you can keep your balance. If enough weight is shifted to one side, the other hand can be raised slowly to the fingertips. Keep the movement controlled, else you could hurt your hand or fingers. The fingertip hand can help to keep the balance and take over a bit weight and therefore make the handstand easier. Keep proper Handstand form. This means Straight arms with elevated shoulders. Posterior Pelvic position. See much weight you can shift to one side safely and in control. Hold that position. Than perform the same on the other side.

Variations: The difficulty of the exercise can be increased by reducing the weight on the supporting/fingertip hand. Therefore you can take away one finger by another, until you are only standing on your active hand.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Body in straight line and hollow position
* Shoulders open, elevated and protracted
* Straight arms
* Whole body in tension

list.name: Wall Fingertip Straddle Handstand
list.description:
The Wall Fingertip Straddle Handstand will force you to shift the most weight to one arm, while still getting some support from the other hand which touches the floor with the fingertips. The Wall will help you to get a feeling for different body positions before you try to balane it freely.

Execution: Walk up into a Wall Handstand. Straddle your legs and shift your weight towards one arm. Therefore bend to one side and drop that leg a bit downwards. You will have to push harder into the ground with this hand and shoulder, so you can keep your balance. If enough weight is shifted to one side, the other hand can be raised slowly to the fingertips. Keep the movement controlled, else you could hurt your hand or fingers. The fingertip hand can help to keep the balance and take over a bit weight and therefore make the handstand easier. Keep proper Handstand form. This means Straight arms with elevated shoulders. Posterior Pelvic position. See much weight you can shift to one side safely and in control. Hold that position. Than perform the same on the other side.

Variations: The difficulty of the exercise can be increased by reducing the weight on the supporting/fingertip hand. Therefore you can take away one finger by another, until you are only standing on your active hand.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Body in straight line and hollow position
* Shoulders open, elevated and protracted
* Straight arms
* Whole body in tension

list.name: Straddle One Arm Handstand
list.description:
With the Straddle One Arm Handstand you will have your whole body weight on one arm. The straddle position helps you to find easier balance as with legs pressed together.

Execution: Go into a handstand. Straddle your legs. Shift your weight to one side. In control move one hand away from the floor. Keep proper Handstand form. This means Straight arms with elevated shoulders. Posterior Pelvic position. Really push hard with your active arm and shoulder, in order to avoid your body from rotating. Use your straddled legs for help to balance.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Body in straight line and hollow position
* Shoulders open, elevated and protracted
* Straight arms
* Whole body in tension

list.name: Diamond One Arm Handstand
list.description:
The Diamond One Arm Handstand is a bit harder than the Straddle One Arm Handstand as it you will have less balancing support with your legs bend.

Execution: Go into a handstand. Straddle your legs and bend them, so your feet touch each other. Your legs should perform a diamond shape. Shift your weight to one side. In control move one hand away from the floor. Keep proper Handstand form. This means Straight arms with elevated shoulders. Posterior Pelvic position. Really push hard with your active arm and shoulder, in order to avoid your body from rotating.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Body in straight line and hollow position
* Shoulders open, elevated and protracted
* Straight arms
* Whole body in tension

list.name: One Arm Handstand
list.description:
The One Arm Handstand is the ultimate expression of Handstand skills. It requires strength, flexibilty, balance and experience. Many people are able to kick up into a banana handstand, hold it for a few seconds and fall over. Starting from this, learning a proper straight line handstand, which can be balanced and hold easily is a great journey. Now imagine doing this on only one arm. This is much more harder. Not many people are able to perform this skill and achieving this is really remarkable.

Execution: Go into a handstand. Squeeze your legs together. Shift your weight to one side. In control move one hand away from the floor. Keep proper Handstand form. This means Straight arms with elevated shoulders. Posterior Pelvic position. Really push hard with your active arm and shoulder, in order to avoid your body from rotating.

Entry: There are many ways to enter a handstand. Here are some possibilities:
1. Kick Up
2. Tucked Press
3. Straddle Press
4. Pike Press
5. Frogstand to Handstand

Mobility: A good mobility will be very benefical for the handstand.
Shoulder Mobility: Shoulder mobility is essential to have a nice stacking of the joints. If you don't have enough shoulder mobility, you will have to compensate that by bending your hip rather than arching your back. Think of a hollow body position and slight posterior pelvic tilt.
Wrist Mobility: All of your weight will be on your wrists, so make sure to properly warm them up. Recovery on joints will take much longer than for muscles, so don't overdue it. If you have issues with your wrists or not enough mobility, you can use Parallets. They will help to reduce the stress on the wrists and less mobility is needed than on the floor.

Hand Placement and Balancing: Main balancing is done with the hands and fingers. Bigger adjustmens can be made through bending the ellbows or pushing through the shoulders. Your fingers can really make a difference so make sure to use them effectively. Your hand will have three touchpoints with the ground. Your bottom palm line, top palm line and your fingers. Your most weight should be on your bottom palm line. By pressing your fingers into the ground you can help yourself from falling over. Also make sure to spread your fingers wide apart, so you have a wider balancing area.

Exit: If you work on a free handstand you also need to know how to exit safely. There different strategies for that.
1. The easiest is to just reverse the kick up, but what to do if you are about to fall over.
2. You can exit sideways before really falling over. A bit like in a cartwheel.
3. Unroll. This needs a bite more control and a forward roll as prerequesite. Pull your head in, bend your arms, tuck your legs and roll over your back.
If you can't exit properly yet, you can also start with an assistend handstand or a wall as help.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Body in straight line and hollow position
* Shoulders open, elevated and protracted
* Straight arms
* Whole body in tension

list.name: Reverse Lunge
list.description:
The Reverse Lunge is a variation of the normal Lunge. Instead of making a step forward, it is done by making a step backward. This change in the exercise is very helpful if you want to reduce the stress on your knees. It will give the knee more stabilization and reduce it moving forward to much. Like with all unilateral exercises, always work on both sides.

Execution: Stand with both feet shoulder wide apart. Take a wide step back and keep your back straight and your chest high. Don't allow your knee to shake, but keep it stable. Your weight should be an the heel of your active leg. Use the backward moving leg only for stabilization. Push from the heel of your active leg and come back to the starting position.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Push through heel
* Keep knee stable
* Straight back
* Chest high

list.name: Narrow Split Squat
list.description:
The Narrow Split Squat is a variation of the Split Squat, which will mimic the Shrimp Squat movement pattern a bit more. Be aware that this variation will put more stress on your knee, as the regular Split Squat.

Execution: It is recommended to start this exercise from the bottom, in order to get the right foot placement and distance. Place the knee of your back leg directly behing the heel of your front leg. With the correct setup start by pushing yourself up from the heel of your front leg. Try to lean as little forward as necessary. Keep your knee stabble, your back straight and chest high. Most of the weight should be on your front leg.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Push through heel
* Keep knee stable
* Straight back
* Chest high
* Knee to heel

list.name: Split Squat
list.description:
The Split Squat is a bit easier than the Lunge, as it removes the moving feet from exercise.

Execution: It is recommended to start this exercise from the bottom, in order to get the right foot placement and distance. The setup is in a 90/90/90 degree position. Your front leg should be bend at 90 degree at the knee. Your back leg should be bend at 90 degree at the knee as aswell. Both legs are in 90 degree to each other at hip level. With the correct setup start by pushing yourself up from the heel of your front leg. Keep the 90 degree with this leg and don't shift forward or backward. Keep your knee stabble, your back straight and chest high. Most of the weight should be on your front leg.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Push through heel
* Keep knee stable
* Straight back
* Chest high
* 90/90/90 degree position

list.name: Supported Shrimp Squat
list.description:
The Supported Shrimp is an intermediate step before you can perform the Shrimp Squat with your arms in front of you. With the back leg you can push with as much support as needed.

Execution: Start in a standing position. Stretch your arms in front of you. Balance on one foot and keep your knee stable the whole time. Slowly and with control lower your other leg backwards, while bending in the knee of the front leg. Keep your back straight the whole time and chest high. When your back leg lays on the ground, push yourself up. Try to keep the most weight on the front leg, but support with the back leg as needed. This means, push from the heel of your front leg and as needed push with the back leg. Bring yourself in a standing position again, while still balancing on one foot.

Variations: If the exercise is too hard, start from the bottom and push yourself up. This way you will remove the eccentric part of the movement, until you have enough strength to do the full thing. Another way to make it easier, is to place an elevation on the ground. When your knee touches the elevation, push yourself up again. This will reduce the range of motion but includes also the important exccentric part of the movement.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Push through heel
* Keep knee stable
* Straight back
* Chest high

list.name: No Arm Shrimp Squat
list.description:
The No Arm Shrimp Squat will remove the support from the back leg completely. Therefore only the knee of your back leg should touch the floor.

Execution: Start in a standing position. Stretch your arms in front of you. Balance on one foot and keep your knee stable the whole time. Slowly and with control lower your other leg backwards, while bending in the knee of the front leg. Keep your back straight the whole time and chest high. Place the knee of your back leg behind the heel of your front leg. When the knee touches the floor, push yourself back up. Keep the heel of your front leg on the floor and push from there. The foot of your bacck leg shouldn't toch the floor.

Variations: If the exercise is too hard, start from the bottom and push yourself up. This way you will remove the eccentric part of the movement, until you have enough strength to do the full thing.

Tipps: If you have to bend forward too much, try to engage your core more.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Push through heel
* Keep knee stable
* Straight back
* Chest high
* Knee to heel

list.name: One Arm Shrimp Squat
list.description:
The One Arm Shrimp Squat uses only one arm as counterbalance and counterweight. You will see that it will require more strength and mobility than with both hands in front of you.

Execution: Start in a standing position. Stretch one arm in front of you and with the other grab your back foot. Balance on one foot and keep your knee stable the whole time. Slowly and with control lower your other leg backwards, while bending in the knee of the front leg. Keep your back straight the whole time and chest high. Place the knee of your back leg behind the heel of your front leg. When the knee touches the floor, push yourself back up. Keep the heel of your front leg on the floor and push from there. The foot of your bacck leg shouldn't toch the floor.

Tipps: If you have to bend forward too much, try to engage your core more.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Push through heel
* Keep knee stable
* Straight back
* Chest high
* Knee to heel
* One hand on back foot

list.name: Shrimp Squat
list.description:
If you think of advanced bodyweight leg exercises, most think of the Pistol Squat. But the Shrimp Squat is also a great skill, which requires a lot of leg strength, balance and mobility. The real Shrim Squat has both hands on your back foot, so there is no arm in front of you for counterbalance or counterweight.

Execution: Start in a standing position. Grab your back foot with both hands. Balance on one foot and keep your knee stable the whole time. Slowly and with control lower your other leg backwards, while bending in the knee of the front leg. Keep your back straight the whole time and chest high. Place the knee of your back leg behind the heel of your front leg. When the knee touches the floor, push yourself back up. Keep the heel of your front leg on the floor and push from there. The foot of your bacck leg shouldn't toch the floor.

Tipps: If you have to bend forward too much, try to engage your core more.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Knee touches floor gently
* Knee directly behind heel
* Push through heel
* Both hands on back foot

list.name: Korean Dip
list.description:
The Korean Dip is a special kind of Dip exercise. It is more demanding for the shoulders and also requires a lot of biceps. Partly it is the same movement pattern as the Hefesto, but limited in the range of motion and therefore much easier and a good start. Nevertheless it is already an intermediate exercise which requires solid strength, especially in the shoulders and arms. Also the exercise is stressfull to the joints, so don't overdue it.


Execution

Step 1: Start by sitting on a bar and grab it with a supinated grip (palms facing forward). Your wrists should be rotate slightly backwards instead forward, so you can keep a strong grip, while descending your body. Arms are straight.

Step 2: Slowly descend with your body, while keeping your back close to the bar and your feet behind yourself. Your back will arch naturally, which is ok. Still keep the tension in your core and don't overarch. Keep your elbows inward, also during the push phase.

Step 3: Bottom position is reached when your arms are in a 90° angle or lower. Hold this position briefly.

Step 4: Push yourself up, while keeping your back close the bar. An arched back is necessary to get the backward rotation around the bar. Also don't loosen your grip and think of rotating it backwards.

Step 5: Back at the top your arms should be straight and your scapula depressed (pushing downwards). Pause there briefly without sitting on the bar and go for the next rep.


Variations

Make it easier:
1. Use a band for assistance.
2. Use a bar where you can support yourself with your legs.
3. Reduce the range of motion. Don't descend to 90° if it is too hard.
4. Use a pronated grip (palm facing backward)

Make it harder:
1. Descend below 90°, as low as you can.
2. Increase the distance between the bar and your back.
3. Perform Archer Korean Dips, which means using a wider grip and descending towards one side.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Strong supinated grip
* Scapula protracted and depressed
* Arms to 90° or lower
* Back close to the bar
* Keep elbows in

list.name: Assisted Hefesto Pull
list.description:
The goal of the Hefesto Pull is to increase your strength for the lower part of the Hefesto movement.


Execution:

Step 1: Starting position. Find a low height bar. Ideally on height with your lower back. Face it with your back and grab it in a supinated grip (palms facing forward). Knee down until your arms a completely straight, support yourself with the help of your legs. Tipp: If there are only higher bars available, find something stable you can stand on, to reduce the gap.

Step 2: Pull your upper back toward the bar. Push with your legs to help. Remain a protracted and depressed scapula as well as a strong grip. Don't flare your elbows out too much as this could lead to joint injuries.

Step 3: When your upper back touches the bar the rep counts. Hold the end position briefly.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Strong supinated grip
* Scapula protracted and depressed
* Arms to 90° or lower

list.name: Hefesto Dip
list.description:
The goal of the Hefesto Dip is to increase your strength for the top part of the Hefesto movement. You can consider it as an extrem Korean Dip or half Hefesto. The difference to the Korean Dip is that you have to lower yourself further down and bring the shoulders lower and forward. Also the distance between your back and the bar will be greater, which will increase the leverage and therefore difficulty.


Execution

Step 1: Start by sitting on a bar and grab it with a supinated grip (palms facing forward). Your wrists should be rotate slightly backwards instead forward, so you can keep a strong grip, while descending your body. Arms are straight.

Step 2: Slowly descend with your body, while keeping your back close to the bar and your feet behind yourself. Your back will arch naturally, which is ok. Still keep the tension in your core and don't overarch.

Step 3: Descend even further by bringing your shoulder and back further away from the bar.

Step 4: Bottom position is reached when your arms have more than 90° angle and your back has some distance to the bar. Hold this position briefly.

Step 5: Push yourself up before you fall. An arched back is necessary to get the backward rotation around the bar. Also don't loosen your grip and think of rotating it backwards.

Step 6: Back at the top your arms should be straight and your scapula depressed (pushing downwards). Pause there briefly without sitting on the bar and go for the next rep.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Strong supinated grip
* Scapula protracted and depressed
* Arms more than 90°

list.name: Skin the Cat (sup.)
list.description:
This Skin the Cat variation is done with a supinated grip (palms facing forward). The change of grip will require more wrist rotation and a firmer grip. Also it will put more load to your biceps as your joints won't be able to help as much as with the pronated grip. To be able to hold this position is necessary to even think about a Hefesto.

Execution: Skin the cat starts in a dead hang position. Pull your legs to the bar and continue rotating them past your elbows, while tucking them towards your chest. Continue the rotation until your toes point to the ground. Try to really extend yourself and carefully stretch your shoulders. In this bottom position your legs should be straight and your arms fully extended. Start the rewind and pull yourself back up.
list.techniqueSummary:
*Firm supinated grip
* Straight arms 
* Scapula protracted 
Keep your core tight

list.name: Negative Hefesto
list.description:
The Negative Hefesto is a great exercise to understand the whole movement pattern of the Hefesto in an excentric way.


Execution:

Step 1: Start by sitting on a bar and grab it with a supinated grip (palms facing forward). Your wrists should be rotate slightly backwards instead forward, so you can keep a strong grip, while descending your body. Arms are straight.

Step 2: Slowly descend with your body, while keeping your back close to the bar and your feet behind yourself. Your back will arch naturally, which is ok. Still keep the tension in your core and don't overarch.

Step 3: Descend even further by bringing your shoulder and back further away from the bar.

Step 4: Lower yourself by slowly extend your arms until they are straight and a German Hang with supinated grip is reached.

Step 5: Sit back on the bar and repeat. A lower bar is recommend for this exercise as you can easy sit back on the bar.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Strong supinated grip
* Scapula protracted and depressed

list.name: Hefesto Pull
list.description:
The Hefesto Pull exercise will work on the bottom phase of the Hefesto. It requires a solid Tuck Back Lever with supinated grip and a lot of pulling strength.


Execution

Step 1: Start in German Hang with supinated grip. This means your palms are facing forward and you are in the bottom position of the Skin the cat exercise.

Step 2: Initiate the movement by depressing and protracting your scapula. This means to push your shoulder blades down and forward. Think of trying to squeeze your shoulder together.

Step 3: Pull hard by bending your arms and try to bring your upper back towards the bar. If you can't bend your arms this exercise is to difficulty for now and you should progress with easier progressions.

Step 4: Try to hold the highest position of your pull before descending.


Variations:
Make it easier: use a resistance band for help or use perform an Assisted Hefesto Pull exercise.
Make it harder: Pull your upper back to touch the bar.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Strong supinated grip
* Scapula protracted and depressed
* Arms more than 90°

list.name: Tuck Back Lever (sup.)
list.description:
The Back Lever with supinated grip (palms facing forward) mimics the bottom part of the Hefesto. The change of grip will require more wrist rotation and a firmer grip. Also it will put more load to your biceps as your joints won't be able to help as much as with the pronated grip.
Execution:
The Tuck Back Lever starts in a dead hang position. Pull your legs to the bar and continue rotating them past your elbows, while tucking them towards your chest. Stop the rotation when your back is parallel to the ground, while keeping your legs tucked towards your chest. The Tuck Back Lever is an easy variation of the Back Lever, because the leverage is highly reduced through the tucking of your legs. Keep your arms straight and protract your scapula by pushing your shoulders forward
list.techniqueSummary:
*Strong supinated grip
* Straight arms 
* Scapula protracted 
* Body parallel to ground 
* Hips on level with shoulders

list.name: Assisted Hefesto
list.description:
The Assisted Hefesto exercise allows you to perform the whole Hefesto movement only with some assistance of a resistance band. The difference to the much more easier Assisted Hefesto Pull exercise is that you won't support yourself with your legs, but only with a band. This will make it much harder as the support is limited. If you don't have a resistance band you can still progress with the progressions on lower levels or you can ask somebody to support you by grabing your feet and help pushing you up.


Execution

Step 1: Prepare the band support. There are 2 ways to use a resistance band for help. 1. you can double it and put it on your hip, while holding it firmly with your hands. 2. Wrap it around the bar and step in with your feet. Resistance bands are available in different strengths. Chooe the one which fits you best and progress to lighter ones.

Step 2: Start by sitting on a bar and grab it with a supinated grip (palms facing forward). Your wrists should be rotate slightly backwards instead forward, so you can keep a strong grip, while descending your body. Arms are straight. Use the resistance band as described in step 1.

Step 3: Slowly descend with your body, while keeping your back close to the bar and your feet behind yourself. Your back will arch naturally, which is ok. Still keep the tension in your core and don't overarch.

Step 4: Descend even further by bringing your shoulder and back further away from the bar.

Step 5: Lower yourself by slowly extend your arms until they are straight and a German Hang with supinated grip is reached.

Step 6: When the bottom position is reached, pull hard by bending your arms and try to bring your upper back towards the bar. Think of pulling the bar towards your arm pits

Step 7: The higher you get you will have to rotate your elbows over the bar. Therefore arching the back will be necessary to some extend.

Step 8: Bring your upper back to touch the bar for the rep to count.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Strong supinated grip
* Pull towards arm pit
* Scapula protracted
* Arched back

list.name: Hefesto
list.description:
The Hefesto is a very difficult exercise. It requires a great amount of strength, especially in the biceps and shoulders. Also be careful with this exercise as it is demands strong joints.


Execution

Step 1: Entry the starting position, which is a German Hang with supinated grip (palms facing forward). 1. entry method is to sit on a bar, grab it with supinated grip and slowly descend until the German Hang position with straight arms is reached. 2 entry method is to start in a dead hang, tucking your legs and bringing the feets behind the bar, so you can put the bar to the back of your knees. This will give you the support to change your grip for the Hefesto. Then you can rotate backwards to the German Hang position.

Step 2: Your wrists should be rotate slightly backwards instead forward, so you can keep a strong grip. Arms are straight.

Step 3: Initiate the movement by depressing and protracting your scapula. This means to push your shoulder blades down and forward. Think of trying to squeeze your shoulder together.

Step 4: Pull hard by bending your arms and try to bring your upper back towards the bar. Think of pulling the bar towards your arm pits

Step 5: The higher you get you will have to rotate your elbows over the bar. Therefore arching the back will be necessary to some extend.

Step 6: Bring your upper back to touch the bar for the rep to count.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Strong supinated grip
* Pull towards arm pit
* Scapula protracted
* Arched back

list.name: Jumping Jacks
list.description:
Jumping Jacks are a basic warum up and conditioning exercise. They will increase your blood flow and loosen up your joints.


Execution

Step 1: Starting position. Bring your legs and feets together. Put your straight arms to the side, with your hands touching your thighs

Step 2: Sligthly jump and spread your legs more than shoulder width apart. At the same time bring your arms up sideways, until your hands touch over your head.

Step 3: Try to land softly on the ball of your foot, compensating the impact. Also keep the tension in your body the whole time and your glutes engaged. Avoid your knees to turn inwards/outwards too much 

Step 4: Jump back into the starting position and again try to land softly.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Engage glutes
* Don't bring your knees inwards/outwards
* Keep the tension in your body

list.name: Burpee
list.description:
Burpees are one of the best conditioning exercises. A burpee basically combines a Push Up with a Jump Squat. It will bring your blood pumping and loosen a lot of joints in your body. Therefore it is also a great Warm Up exercise.


Execution

Step 1: Start by performing a Squat. Keep your core and glutes engeaged. Back is straight and your feet about shoulder-width apart.

Step 2: Place your hands directly in front of you and shift the weight towards them.

Step 3: Kick your feet back and softly land in a Plank position

Step 4: Perform a Push Up (if this is too hard, just skip this step)

Step 5: Jump your feet back behind your hands. A good hip mobility is importantant for this step.

Step 6: Stand up and reach your arms over your head.

Step 7: Jump into the air and land softy into the starting position
list.techniqueSummary:
* Core engaged
* Glutes engaged
* Don't arch back

list.name: Arch Active Hang
list.description:
The Arch Active Hang is a great exercise for scapula retraction (pulling your shoulder blades backwards) with straight arms. Scapula retraction strengths will convert greatly for exercises like Front Lever or Pull Ups. Nevertheless it is performed quite differently, as you legs should be kept hanging. Contrary to a Front Lever this will allow you to focus more on the retraction instead. A very important aspect of this exercise is to engage your glutes to stabilize your spine. Being able to arch your back is important for every movement athlete, but if you have any issues in your lower back while doing this exercises, don't do it.


Execution

Step 1: Start in an Active Hang and keep your glutes engaged.

Step 2: Keep your arms straight and try to pull your hands towards your belly, while pulling your shoulder blades backwards as hard as you can. Arch your back and keep your glutes engaged. Keep your legs apart and hanging. 

Step 3: The goal is to bring your hip as high as possible by arching and retracting back. Try to hold the top position for a while.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Scapula retracted
* Engage glutes
* Keep legs hanging
* Straight arms
* Chest open
* Arched back

list.name: Arm Circles
list.description:
The Arm Circles are a good warm up exercise for the mobility of your shoulder joints.


Execution

Step 1: Start in a standing position with your arms straight and by your side.

Step 2: Slowly rotate your straight arms forward by making big circles. With poor shoulder mobility the arms will flare to the side. With better shoulder mobility you can try to keep your arms more inward. Keep your core engaged during this exercise

Step 3: If you are comfortable you can increase the speed of the rotation.

Step 4: Stop the rotation and reverse it. Swing your straight arms backwards.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Straight arms
* Core engaged

list.name: Torso Twist
list.description:
Torso Twists are a great mobility warm up exercise for the back.


Execution

Step 1: Start in a standing position with your feet more than shoulder wide apart.

Step 2: Lean your upper body forward until it is parallel to the ground. Keep your back straight.

Step 3: Rotate the left hand towards your right foot. Rotate your right hand towards the sky, right above your head. Keep your arms straight during the whole movement.

Step 4: Switch it up. Right hand to left foot and left hand to the sky.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Straight arms
* Straight back
* Feet more than shoulder wide apart

list.name: Wrist Roll
list.description:
A lot of Calisthenics skills will require a lot of wrist strength. Proper warm up for Handstands or Planche progressions are a must. The Wrist Rolls are a great way to start with.


Execution

Step 1: Bring your hands together and interlace your fingers

Step 2: Slowly and fluidly roll your hands in every direction.

Step 3: Increase the speed if comrortable and also reverse the rotation.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Interlace fingers
* Stretch without pain

list.name: Cat / Cow
list.description:
The Cat / Cow exercise is known from Yoga. It is a nice warm up and mobility exercise for the spine and back.


Execution

Step 1: Start in a Table Top position. Hands, knees and feet on ground. Place your shoulders right over your hands. Place your hip over your knees. This way you will have a nice 90/90/90 degree position.

Step 2: Bring yourself into the Cow position. Therefore retract your shoulders back and let your belly fall towards the floor. Making a nice arch in the back. Gaze up to extend the arch.

Step 3: Push yourself up into the Cat position. Therefore protract your shoulders by pushing into the floor and rounding your back. Tuck your head in to extend the rounding.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Shoulders over wrist
* Hips over knees

list.name: Bridge
list.description:
The Bridge is a great exercise to increase your mobility and flexibility. It targets your the muscles on the back of your body (posterior chain) and stretches the muscles on the front of your body (anterior chain). It requires a lot of mobility in your shoulders and hip flexors.


Execution

Step 1: Lay on your back. Bring your feet close to your glutes. Place your hands next to the ears.

Step 2: Push your hip up. Keep the weight on your hands and feets, not the head.

Step 3: Try to straighten your arms and arching in your back. Bend in the shoulder, back and hip. Not only in one spot.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Feet and hands shoulder width apart
* Lift hip
* Extend arms

list.name: Squat Reach
list.description:
The Squat Reach is a great stretching exercise, which targets ankle, hip and back mobility.


Execution

Step 1: Go into a deep squat position. Feet are at least shoulder width apart. Back is straight. Knees go outward and don't turn inwards. Weight is on the heels, don't raise them. If you don't have the necessary mobility yet, try to spread your legs wider or go into a higher squat hold.

Step 2: Put one hand on the foot. With the other hand reach to the sky and follow your gaze with it. Avoid your knees from turning inwards and actively push them outwards. Stay as upright as possible.

Step 3: Rest a bit in this position until you swith hands and stretch to the other side.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Knees push outward
* Straight back

list.name: Elbow Circles
list.description:
To prepare the elbow joints is very important for many Calisthenics exercises. Lack of proper warm up can e.g. result in golfer elbow issues, which can be very stubborn. The Elbow Circles are a great and easy way to warm up the elbow joints.


Execution

Step 1: Start in a standing position with enoug space around. Raise your elbows sideways.

Step 2: Rotate both forearms in circles

Step 3: Repeat the circles in counter direction
list.techniqueSummary: * Elbows bent

list.name: Seated Forward Bend
list.description:
The Forward Bend will stretch especially your hamstrings, which is very important for e.g. Handstand Press or V-Sit. Please consider: Don't overstretch or push through pain. Low intensity stretching show better results in range of motion than moderate or high intensity stretching.


Execution

Step 1: Sit on the floor with your legs straight. Keep your back straight and flex the toes towards yourself.

Step 2: Lean forward while keeping a straight back. Grab your feet with the hands and bend the knees as needed.

Step 3: Straighten the legs till you feel a painfree stretch in the hamstrings.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Flex Toes
* Belly to thighs
* Bend in hip not spine
* Scapula depressed

list.name: Standing Forward Bend
list.description:
The Forward Bend will stretch especially your hamstrings, which is very important for e.g. Handstand Press or V-Sit. Please consider: Don't overstretch or push through pain. Low intensity stretching show better results in range of motion than moderate or high intensity stretching.


Execution

Step 1: Start in a standing position. Back and legs straight.

Step 2: Bend from your hip and try to maintain a straight back till you can.

Step 3: Grab your legs or even feet if you can and hold that position. Feel the stretch in your hamstrings, not the lower back. If you feel more stretch in your spine, you are doing it wrong. Your weight should be mostly on the heels and the back straight as possible. Also it is ok to have bend knees at the beginning.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Belly to thighs
* Bend in hip not spine
* Scapula depressed

list.name: Pigeon
list.description:
The Pigeon stretch is a great exercise for hip extension and external hip rotation. Please consider: Don't overstretch or push through pain. Low intensity stretching show better results in range of motion than moderate or high intensity stretching.


Execution

Step 1: Start in a plank position. Place the heel of the right foot to the left hand.

Step 2: Slowly place the knee of your right leg towards the right hand. Your Knee should be now ben to about 90°. The left leg is streched backwards and as straight as possible.

Step 3: Now slowly try to bring your hip towards the ground and push your left leg further behind. Keep your upper body tall. This way you will stretch the rotation of your front leg and the extension of back leg.

Step 4: Hold the position and afterwads switch legs.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Heel and knee on same level
* Knee 90° angle
* Upper body tall

list.name: Butterfly
list.description:
The Butterfly stretches your groins, inner thighs and knees. Please consider: Don't overstretch or push through pain. Low intensity stretching show better results in range of motion than moderate or high intensity stretching.


Execution

Step 1: Sit on the floor and bring your feet together with bended knees. Keep the back straight and core tight.

Step 2: Bring the heels as close to your body as possible and push your knees towards the floor.

Step 3: Hold that position. If you want you can help with your arms to press the legs closer to the floor.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Back straight
* Heels together and in
* Knees push towards floor

list.name: Pancake
list.description:
The Pancake is a great stretch which will benefit to a lot of skills with straddle leg position or core compression like the V-sit. Please consider: Don't overstretch or push through pain. Low intensity stretching show better results in range of motion than moderate or high intensity stretching.


Execution

Step 1: Start in a sitting position with your legs spread over 90° and your back straight. If you can't do this, you should work on the Butterfly stretch or Middle Split stretches.

Step 2: Slowly bring your upper body towards the floor through bending from the hip. Keep a straight back by doing a anterior pelvic tilt. This means rotate your hip forward instead allowing it to rotate back and round your back.

Step 3: At your lowest point hold this position and breath into it. At exhale you can try to push a bit deeper into the stretch. Also it helps if you can hold yourself onto something, so you don't have activly to fight against gravity that hard.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Straight back
* Straight legs
* Anterior pelvic tilt

list.name: Spiderman Stretch
list.description:
The Spiderman Stretch targets will open your hip and groin. Please consider: Don't overstretch or push through pain. Low intensity stretching show better results in range of motion than moderate or high intensity stretching.


Execution

Step 1: Start in a plank position and bring the right foot beside the right hand

Step 2: With your right arm push your knee out. Keep your core and glute engaged and push your hip toward the floor while keeping your chest high.

Step 3: Hold the position, then switch sides.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Knee outwards
* Hip towards floor

list.name: Spiderman Stretch with rotation
list.description:
The Spiderman Stretch targets will open your hip and groin. The extra rotation will help your T-spine mobility. Please consider: Don't overstretch or push through pain. Low intensity stretching show better results in range of motion than moderate or high intensity stretching.


Execution

Step 1: Start in a plank position and bring the right foot beside the right hand

Step 2: With your right arm push your knee out. Keep your core and glute engaged and push your hip toward the floor while keeping your chest high.

Step 3: Hold the position briefly, then bring your left arm towards the sky and follow your hand with your head.

Step 4: Hold this position also for a few seconds and then switch sides.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Knee outwards
* Hip towards floor

list.name: Shoulder Extension
list.description:
There are multiple ways to stretch shoulder extension. We start with the basic seated shoulder extension. Please consider: Don't overstretch or push through pain. Low intensity stretching show better results in range of motion than moderate or high intensity stretching.


Execution

Step 1: Sit on the floor and place the hands behind you with the fingers pointing back. Keep your chest up, but don't arch your back. Push your shoulder blades down and back.

Step 2: Either lift your hip and bring it forward or walk your hands further backwards.

Step 3: Hold the position when you feel a nice stretch in your shoulder. To increase the stretch you can straighten your legs
list.techniqueSummary:
* Don't arch back
* Shoulder blades down and back
* Chest up

list.name: Wall Downdog
list.description:
The Wall Downdog is a great stretch for shoulder flexion. This shoulder flexibility is greatly responsible to straighten your Handstand. Please consider: Don't overstretch or push through pain. Low intensity stretching show better results in range of motion than moderate or high intensity stretching.


Execution

Step 1: Find a wall or table and put your hands on it. Lower your straight upper body until it parallel to the floor. Your body should be now in a L-shape. Or if the table is lower you should bend your knees, so your upper body is still parallel to the ground.

Step 2: Our goal is to stretch the shoulder flexion, so try to push your chest towards the floor, while keeping a straight back and straight arms.

Step 3: Hold the position, but don't arch your back or bring your shoulders towards your ears. This would decrease the stretch. Also we are not trying to primarily stretch the hamstrings here, so bend your knees as needed.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Straight arms
* Flat back

list.name: Twisted Downward Dog
list.description:
Our primarily goal with the Twisted Downward Dog is to stretch the back and increase the T-spine mobility. Please consider: Don't overstretch or push through pain. Low intensity stretching show better results in range of motion than moderate or high intensity stretching.


Execution

Step 1: Start in a Downward Dog position with your legs spread apart. Keep your arms straight and open your shoulders. Remember our primarily goal is to stretch the back, so it is ok to bend the knees if the only stretch you feel is in your hamstrings.

Step 2: Increase the pressing from your left hand into the floor. Release your right hand and grab your left foot with it. If you can't, try to grab higher on to your leg or bring it as close to the foot as you can.

Step 3: Increase the twist by looking under your armpit. Hold that position and then switch sides
list.techniqueSummary:
* Straight arms
* Open shoulders
* Legs spread apart

list.name: Middle Split
list.description:
The Middle Split stretch will help you with you all straddle exercises. Please consider: Don't overstretch or push through pain. Low intensity stretching show better results in range of motion than moderate or high intensity stretching.


Execution

Step 1: For this stretch it can help if you have somethign to hold yourself. A wall or couch table. Start in a wide stance  and slowly lower yourself down.

Step 2: When you feel you can get lower, try to rotate your hip backward (anterior pelvic tilt). Also try to rotate your knees outwards and point your toes sideways. With these adjustments you should be able to get even lower.

Step 3: Hold the bottom position.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Rotate hip backward
* Rotate knees outward

list.name: Front Split
list.description:
The Front Split is a good stretch for your hamstrings and thighs. Please consider: Don't overstretch or push through pain. Low intensity stretching show better results in range of motion than moderate or high intensity stretching.


Execution

Step 1: Start in a wide Lunge position. Place your hands on the floor or a support if available.

Step 2: Push your straight fornt leg forward and your bend back leg backwards, while descending your hip. Keep your back straight.

Step 3: Hold the bottom position keep everything square.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Hip faces forward
* Feet and hip in one line

list.name: Frog Stretch
list.description:
The Frog Stretch will greatly beneftig your lower body mobility and Squat exercises, as it helps to improve hip opening. Please consider: Don't overstretch or push through pain. Low intensity stretching show better results in range of motion than moderate or high intensity stretching.


Execution

Step 1: Start with your hands and knees on the floor. Place the knees already wide apart. Keep your back straight.

Step 2: Sink your torso towards the ground. Push your knees wider apart and sink with your hip towards the heels.

Step 3: Hold that position. Now come forward and bring your hip in front of your knees and towards the ground. Hold that position as well.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Wide stance
* Neutral back (PPT)

list.name: Towel Pull Up
list.description:
The Towel Pull Up is a beginner exercise which will mimic the motion pattern of a Pull Up, but requires a fraction of the strength. Still it will help you to learn the muscular coordination and engage your back muscles without much load.


Execution

Step 1: Lay down on the ground with your front side. Start with your straight arms in front of you. Grab a towel or band with a grip that is a bit more than shoulder wide apart. Squeeze the towel or band outwards to keep the tension.

Step 2: Raise your head (optionally the legs) and chest above the ground and hold it there. Pull your shoulder blades down (depress) and back (retract) during the execution.

Step 3: Pull the towel/band towards your chest and under your head. Squeeze your back muscles and pull your elbows back. Hold this position for a brief time.

Step 4: Bring your arms forward to the starting position, until they are straight again. Keep the towel/band under tension.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Squeeze Towel outwards
* Chest above ground
* Arched back
* Shoulder blades depressed and retracted

list.name: Lying Arm Circles
list.description:
The Lying Arm Circles are an easy exercise to get your back muscles engaged and warmed up.


Execution

Step 1: Lay down on the ground with your front side. Start with your straight arms in front of you. Fingertips touching each other.

Step 2: Make a big half circle with your arms, while keeping them straight. Palms facing the floor. At about half the cirlce you will need to rotate your arms, so your palms face the sky. Try to keep them as straight as possible. The circle is finished when the fingertips touch above your butt. The movement should be slow and in control.

Step 3: In a reverse motion, bring your arms into the starting position right in front of you.

Step 1: 


Variation

To increase the difficulty you can put a small weight into one hand, which is exchanged between the hands on each rep. Check to exchange it in both directions.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Head above ground
* Shoulder blades depressed and retracted

list.name: Assisted Pull Up
list.description:
The Pull Up is a basic calisthenic exercise, but isn't possible to perform for everybody without some training. The Assisted Pull Up is a great way to learn the Pull Up.

Execution:

Step 1: Knot a band across the bar. Choose a resistance which will give you the right support to reach the required rep in your workout.

Step 2: With one foot step into the band. With your hands grab the bar and if you like also step in the band with your second foot.

Step 3: The exercise starts in a dead hang. The movement is initialized through depressing and retracting the scapula. Next you pull yourself up by engaging your biceps and back muscles.

Step 4: Pull till your chin surpasses the bar (even better till your chest touches the bar).

Step 5: Descend slowly and controlled into a dead hang position. It is important to always perform the full range of motion.

Variations:
The Chin-Up is done with an underhand grip in contrast to the overhand grip of the Pull-Up. With the underhand grip your elbows naturally bend more inwards, by what your biceps will be targeted to a greater extent. Instead of bars you can perform the Pull-Up also on rings.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Scapula depressed and retracted
* No swing 
* Full range of motion (ROM)

list.name: Negative Pull Up
list.description:
The Negative Pull Up is a great exercise to help you achive your first Pull Ups. As eccentric exercise it will help you to utilize more than 100% of your strength.


Execution

Step 1: Find either a lower bar where you can start in the top position of a Pull Up or find something you can step on to reach the top position. Start with a grip wider than shoulders apart. Chin over bar, but with neutral position (look forward not into the sky). Pull the bar down, engage your back and keep your elbows in.

Step 2: Remove the support from your feet and slowly descent with an even speed. Keep your shoulder blades down. Descent till your arms are completely straight. Hold this bottom position briefly. Only now you are allowed to let your shoulder come up into a stretch.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Slowly descent with even tempo
* Shoulder blades down and back
* Full range of motion (ROM)

list.name: Pull Up +5Kg
list.description:
The Pull Up is a basic calisthenic exercise. The difficulty can be increased with some more technically demanding progressions, or by adding some weight. The weight can be added with a weight vest or weight belt.

Execution:
Put your vest or weight belt on. The exercise starts in a dead hang. The movement is initialized through depressing and retracting the scapula. Next you pull yourself up by engaging your biceps and back muscles. Pull till your chin surpasses the bar (even better till your chest touches the bar). Descend slowly and controlled into a dead hang position. It is important to always perform the full range of motion. For weighted Pull Ups it is also important to perform it with a hollow body instead an arched back.

Variations:
The Chin-Up is done with an underhand grip in contrast to the overhand grip of the Pull-Up. With the underhand grip your elbows naturally bend more inwards, by what your biceps will be targeted to a greater extent. Instead of bars you can perform the Pull-Up also on rings.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Scapula depressed
* Hollow body
* No swing
