* Hollow body
* No swing

list.name: Towel Row
list.description:
The Towel Row is an exercise which can be done almost everywhere and can be done for beginners. Before doing this exercise you must ensure that every object (door, pole, towel, band, ...) you are using is stable enough


Execution

Step 1: Find a stable door handle or pole. Knot the towel/band across it. Ensure the stability.

Step 2: Grab the towel/band with straight arms. Go into a seated position and lean back a bit, but keep your back straight.

Step 3: Press your shoulder blades down and retract them during the movement. Bring the towel/band towards your stomach and pull your elbows close and back. Hold the end position a bit. 

Step 4: With control straighten your arms again into the starting position.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Straight back
* Shoulder blades down and back
* Elbows close and back

list.name: Body Row 45°
list.description:
The Body Row is a great back exercise. The Body Row 45° variation requires a bar below chest height. For the regular Body Row the bar should be under hip height, but this variation will make it easier.


Execution

Step 1: Grab the bar wider than shoulders apart. You can change the grip, but pronated is the default. Put your feet below the bar and keep your body and legs in a straight line.

Step 2: Lean back and straighten your arms. Keep your chest high and your shoulder blades neutral or slightly retracted.

Step 3: Pull your chest towards the bar and squeeze your back muscles (retract your shoulder blades). Keep your shoulder blades depressed and don't allow them to come up and round your back. Keep your elbows in and pull them back.

Step 4: Hold the top position briefly and always keep your body in a straight line.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Straight body
* Scapula depressed and retracted
* Elbows in

list.name: Table Row
list.description:
The Table Row exercise is basically a Body Row, but you can perform it without a bar, a stable table is enoug. As alternative you can use two stable chairs and place one to your left and one to your right. Grab them on the inner rest. It won't work with every chair, ensure that they are stable enough. This variation can be a bit harder than the regular body row, as it will require more grip strength.


Execution

Step 1: Lay down below the table. Grab the table plate on the short side. Your lower chest should be below your hands. Keep the body in a straight line. If your feet have bad grip you can bend your legs, but keep your hip high, so the rest of your body will keep the straight line.

Step 2: Lean back and straighten your arms. Keep your chest high and your shoulder blades neutral or slightly retracted.

Step 3: Pull your chest towards the table and squeeze your back muscles (retract your shoulder blades). Keep your shoulder blades depressed and don't allow them to come up and round your back. Keep your elbows in and pull them back.

Step 4: Hold the top position briefly and always keep your body in a straight line.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Straight body
* Scapula depressed and retracted
* Elbows in

list.name: Adv. Body Row
list.description:
This Body Row variation will increase the difficulty by placing your feet on higher ground, which will result into more load you will have to row.


Execution

Step 1: Find a bar below hip height and something like a box where you can put your feet on.

Step 2: Grab the bar wider than shoulders apart. You can change the grip, but pronated is the default. Put your feet on the e.g. box and keep your body and legs in a straight line. The bar should be in front of your lower chest.

Step 2: Lean back and straighten your arms. Keep your chest high and your shoulder blades neutral or slightly retracted.

Step 3: Pull your chest towards the bar and squeeze your back muscles (retract your shoulder blades). Keep your shoulder blades depressed and don't allow them to come up and round your back. Keep your elbows in and pull them back.

Step 4: Hold the top position briefly and always keep your body in a straight line.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Straight body
* Scapula depressed and retracted
* Elbows in

list.name: Archer Row
list.description:
The Archer Row is a great way to increase the difficulty of a Body Row, by making it a unilateral exercise.


Execution

Step 1: Find a bar below hip height. Grab the bar with a wide grip. Keep your body and legs in a straight line. The bar should be in front of your lower chest.

Step 2: Lean back and straighten your arms. Keep your chest high and your shoulder blades neutral or slightly retracted.

Step 3: Pull your chest towards one side of the bar by bending your active arm and squeeze your back muscles (retract your shoulder blades). Try to keep your passive arm as straight as possible. Keep your shoulder blades depressed and don't allow them to come up and round your back. Keep your elbows in and pull them back.

Step 4: Hold the top position briefly and always keep your body in a straight line.

Step 5: Train both sides
list.techniqueSummary:
* Straight body
* Scapula depressed and retracted
* Elbows in

list.name: L Lever Row
list.description:
With the L Lever Row you will have to row your full weight, while also forcing your lats to pull extra in order to keep your hip high.


Execution

Step 1: Grab the bar with a shoulder wide grip. Tuck your legs and bring your feet below the bar. Keep the legs close to the bar and stretch them. The bar should be at your shins. Keep your hip high and back parallel to the ground. Getting into this starting position will already require power.

Step 2: Keep this L shape position while performing the movement. Think about back parallel to the ground, legs straight and close to the bar. Pull yourself up as high as you can, while guiding the bar along the legs. If you keep the L-shape this is a good measurement on how high you can go. You have to bend your arms, pulling your elbows back, while keeping them in.

Step 3: Try to hold the top position briefly. In perfect form you will bring the bar towards your stomach.

Step 4: While descending and stretching your arms, again guide the bar along the legs and keep the L-shape.
list.techniqueSummary:
* L-shape
* Back parallel to the ground
* Elbows in
* Straight legs

list.name: Tuck Front Lever Row
list.description:
The Tuck Front Lever Row is a quite hard rowing exercise. You will have to pull your whole bodyweight, while also forcing your lats to pull extra in order to keep your hip high. If available choose rings to perform this exercise. This is more optimal, as the rings will allow you to pull even higher.


Execution

Step 1: Grab the bar with a shoulder wide grip. Tuck your legs and bring your feet below the bar. Start with your arms straight.

Step 2: Keep the back parallel to the ground and the legs tucked close. Pull yourself up as high as you can, until your shin touches the bar. You have to bend your arms, pulling your elbows back, while keeping them in. Try to hold the top position briefly. 

Step 3: While descending and stretching the arms, keep your back parallel to the ground and complete the rep in a Tuck Front Lever.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Back parallel to the ground
* Elbows in
* Scapula retracted and depressed

list.name: Adv. Tuck Front Lever Row
list.description:
The Advanced Tuck Front Lever Row is a hard rowing exercise. You will have to pull your whole bodyweight, while also forcing your lats to pull extra in order to keep your hip high.


Execution

Step 1: Grab the bar with a shoulder wide grip. Tuck your legs and bring your feet below the bar. Start with your arms straight. Open your tuck till your knees are behind the bar, so when you pull the bar can be guided along your thighs.

Step 2: Keep the back parallel to the ground and your legs in the 90° position. Pull yourself up as high as you can, and guide the bar along your thighs. You have to bend your arms, pulling your elbows back, while keeping them in. Try to hold the top position briefly. In a perfect form, row the bar towards your stomach.

Step 3: While descending and stretching the arms, keep your back parallel to the ground and complete the rep in an Advanced Tuck Front Lever.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Back parallel to the ground
* Elbows in
* Scapula retracted and depressed
* Legs in 90°

list.name: Body Row +20Kg
list.description:
Execution:
Adjust a weight belt or weight vest with 20Kg. Alternatively you can place the 20Kg plate on your stomach. Find a bar which isn't too low or high, around hip height. Rest the feet in front you and keep a straight body. Pull your shoulders back engage your back and biceps to pull yourself up. Your chest should touch the bar.
Variations: You can adjust the difficulty with the bar height. If your body is closer to horizontal the harder the exercise gets. You can make the exercise easier by bending your legs. Also you can use rings instead of a bar or even perform the exercise under a table.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Straight body
* Scapula retracted

list.name: Straddle Front Lever Row
list.description:
The Straddle Front Lever Row is a hard rowing exercise. You will have to pull your whole bodyweight, while also forcing your lats to pull extra in order to keep your hip high.


Execution

Step 1: Grab the bar with a shoulder wide grip. Spread your legs and keep them and your body parallel to the ground. Start with your arms straight.

Step 2: Pull yourself up as high as you can. You have to bend your arms, pulling your elbows back, while keeping them in. Try to hold the top position briefly. In a perfect form, row the bar towards your stomach.

Step 3: While descending and stretching the arms, keep your body parallel to the ground and complete the rep in an Straddle Front Lever.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Back parallel to the ground
* Elbows in
* Scapula retracted and depressed

list.name: Body Row +30Kg
list.description:
Execution:

Rest the feet in front you and keep a straight body. Pull your shoulders back engage your back and biceps to pull yourself up. Your chest should touch the bar.

list.name: Front Lever Row
list.description:
The Front Lever Row is a hard rowing exercise. You will have to pull your whole bodyweight, while also forcing your lats to pull extra in order to keep your body straight and parallel to the ground.


Execution

Step 1: Grab the bar with a shoulder wide grip. Raise yourself into a Front Lever, where your body is parallel to the ground. Start with your arms straight.

Step 2: Pull yourself up as high as you can. You have to bend your arms, pulling your elbows back, while keeping them in. Try to hold the top position briefly. In a perfect form, row the bar towards your stomach.

Step 3: While descending and stretching the arms, keep your body parallel to the ground and complete the rep in a Front Lever.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Body parallel to the ground
* Elbows in
* Scapula retracted and depressed

Rest the feet in front you and keep a straight body. Pull your shoulders back engage your back and biceps to pull yourself up. Your chest should touch the bar.
Variations: You can adjust the difficulty with the bar height. If your body is closer to horizontal the harder the exercise gets. You can make the exercise easier by bending your legs. Also you can use rings instead of a bar or even perform the exercise under a table.

list.name: Knee Push Up
list.description:
A simple way to make a regular Push Up easier, is to perform it on the knees. The Knee Push Up shortens the lever and weight you need to push up


Execution

Step 1: Go on your knees. Place the hands a bit wider than shoulders apart in front of you. Wrist, elbow and shoulder should be stacked. Keep a straight upper body with a slight posterior pelvic tilt. The shoulder blades are depressed and protracted.

Step 1: Lower yourself till your chest touches the floor. Keep your elbows in (45° from the body is optimal).

Step 3: Push yourself up until your arms are completely straight.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Shoulder blades depressed and protracted
* Posterior pelvic tilt
* Straight upper body
* Elbows in

list.name: Push Up +10Kg
list.description:
The Push Up is a basic exercise to build chest and triceps strength. Increase the intensity by adding a weight vest or weight plates.

Execution:
Put a weight vest on or let a partner put weight plates on your back. Start in a plank position with your arms a bit more than shoulder wide apart. Your hands should be in line with your shoulders. Press your shoulders down (proctracted) and back (depressed). Turn your pelves in and keep a good tension in your whole body. Slowly lower yourself down and don't allow your elbows to turn widely outwards. Before your chest almost touches the ground, feel how your shoulder blades squeeze together and push yourself back up.
Variations: Instead of a weight vest or weight plates, quite a lot of load can be added if a partner can lays on your back.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Keep elbows in
* Scapula protracted
* Till pelves backward
* Tight body
* Full range of motion (ROM)
* Don't arch back

list.name: Wide Push Up
list.description:
The Wide Push Up is a variation which is a bit harder than the regular Push Up. The wide grip will target the chest more than the triceps.

Execution:
Start in a plank position with your arms wide apart. Your hands should be in line with your shoulders. Press your shoulders down (proctracted) and back (depressed). Turn your pelves in and keep a good tension in your whole body. Slowly lower yourself down and don't allow your elbows to turn widely outwards. Before your chest almost touches the ground, feel how your shoulder blades squeeze together and push yourself back up.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Scapula protracted
* Till pelves backward
* Tight body
* Full range of motion (ROM)
* Don't arch back

list.name: Plyo Push Up
list.description:
The Plyo Push Up is an explosive variation of the Push Up.


Execution

Step 1: Start in a plank position. Posterior pelvic tilt. Scapula protracted and depressed. Straight body line.

Step 2: Lower yourself to the ground and keep the elbows in (about 45° from the body).

Step 3: Explosively push yourself up and stretch your arms, even fingertips, until they leave the ground.

Step 4: This is important, catch the lift off cautiously and control the downward movement. Use it as charge for the next rep. Speed and timing is important.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Keep elbows in
* Scapula protracted
* Till pelves backward
* Tight body
* Full range of motion (ROM)
* Don't arch back

list.name: Back Clap Push Up
list.description:
The Back Clap Push Up is an explosive variation of the Push Up. You will need enough height, to get the time to clap your hands behind your back. Be cautious and do not injure yourself during this movement. Always make sure you have enough time to catch yourself with the hands in front of you.


Execution

Step 1: Start in a plank position. Posterior pelvic tilt. Scapula protracted and depressed. Straight body line.

Step 2: Lower yourself to the ground and keep the elbows in (about 45° from the body).

Step 3: Explosively push yourself up and stretch your arms, even fingertips, until they leave the ground.

Step 5: If the push elevated your upper body high enough, this will give a bit time to quickly clap the hands behind your back and again quickly bring them in front of you to catch yourself.

Step 5: This is important, catch the lift off cautiously and control the downward movement. Use it as charge for the next rep. Speed and timing is important.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Keep elbows in
* Scapula protracted
* Till pelves backward
* Tight body
* Full range of motion (ROM)
* Don't arch back

list.name: Superman Push Up
list.description:
The Superman Push Up is an explosive variation of the Push Up. You will need enough height, to get the time to raise your arms in front of you, mimicing a superman pose. Be cautious and do not injure yourself during this movement. Always make sure you have enough time to catch yourself.


Execution

Step 1: Start in a plank position. Posterior pelvic tilt. Scapula protracted and depressed. Straight body line.

Step 2: Lower yourself to the ground and keep the elbows in (about 45° from the body).

Step 3: Explosively push yourself up and stretch your arms, even fingertips, until they leave the ground. Also load your feets and kick them up strong.

Step 5: If the push elevated your body high enough, this will give a bit time to quickly stretch your arms in front of you and quickliy bring them back below you to catch yourself. Furthermore your legs need to be kicked up as about the same height, or a bit higher than your arms to keep your balance and mimicing the superman pose.

Step 5: This is important, catch the lift off cautiously and control the downward movement. Use it as charge for the next rep. Speed and timing is important.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Keep elbows in
* Scapula protracted
* Till pelves backward
* Tight body
* Full range of motion (ROM)
* Don't arch back

list.name: Tuck Planche Push Up
list.description:
The Tuck Planche Push Up requires to push your full bodyweight without support. Additional effort is needed to keep the hip high and maintain the stabilization.


Execution

Step 1: Start in a Tuck Planche position. Arms straight, knees tucked, hip on level with shoulders, as scapula protracted and depressed

Step 2: Lower yourself in control while keeping the hip on level with the shoulders. Go below a 90° bend of the arms.

Step 3: From the bottom position push yourself up into the Tuck Planche again and avoid to break the form by lowering the hip.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Straight arms 
* Elbows locked out 
* Scapula protracted 
* Body parallel to ground 
* Legs tucked in

list.name: Pike Walk
list.description:
The Pike Walk is a good exercise for Beginners to learn shoulder stability and some controlled movement under small load. It is a preperation for Pike Push Ups and Handstand oriented exercises.


Execution

Step 1: Start in a plank position. Keep your core engaged. Scapula elevated and protracted.

Step 2: Perform small steps with your hands, under straight arms and bring them closer to the feet. At the same time keep the legs as straight as possible.

Step 3: Ideally the hands touch the feet, if you have enough hamstring flexibility.

Step 4: Again with smal steps from the hands, walk out into the starting Plank position.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Shoulder blades elevated
* Straight arms and legs

list.name: Tuck to Handstand
list.description:
The Tuck to Handstand is an impressive entry into the Handstand, but will require some strength. It can be performed with bending the arms, but is harder with completely straight arms.


Execution

Step 1: Start in a Tuck Planche. Arms straight, legs tucked, scapula protracted and depressed.

Step 1: Push the hip up and try to rotate them directly over your head. At the same time you can lean forward a bit and bend the arms as little as possible, which will help you to get the hip above your head.

Step 2: At the right moment push and stretch the arms and at the same time stretch the legs.

Step 3: Finish into a Handstand with elevated shoulder blades, straight arms and legs.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Shoulder blades protracted and elevated
* Posterior pelvic tilt

list.name: Deep Handstand Push Up
list.description:
The Deep Handstand Push Up will increase the range of motion of the regular Handstand Push Up, but will require some Paralletes or elevation.


Execution

Step 1: Start in a Handstand and descend controlled by keeping a straight body and leaning forward (a hollow body needs more strength than an arched back). Don't stop when your head passed your hands, but go even deeper. Keep your elbows in to have it clean and in control.

Step 2: Push yourself up and keep the balance and straight body line. Finish the rep in a Handstand position.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Elbows in
* Scapula protracted
* Straight body

list.name: Bench Dip
list.description:
The Bench Dip is a great beginner exercise which requires only equipment which can be found anywhere: a chair, bench or any kind of stable elevation with the right height. The higher the easier it gets, the lower the harder. But it should have enough height to allow full range of motion which is at an arm bend of about 90°.


Execution

Step 1: Plance your hands on the elevation. Start position is with arms and legs straight, upper body perpendicular to the floor, butt close to the elvation and hands about shoulder wide apart.

Step 2: Lower yourself down by bending the arms, but keeping the elbows in (close to the body). Go to at least a 90° bend in the arms. Keep the shoulder blades depressed (press them down) and do NOT protracted them (allow them to rotate inwards).

Step 3: Push yourself up until your arms are straight again. Also keep your upper body straight during th whole movement and your hip down.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Elbows in
* Scapula depressed and retracted
* Upper body straight and perpendicular to floor

list.name: Adv. Bench Dip
list.description:
The Advanved Bench Dip is a great beginner exercise which requires only equipment which can be found anywhere: a chair, bench or any kind of stable elevation with the right height. The higher the easier it gets, the lower the harder. But it should have enough height to allow full range of motion which is at an arm bend of about 90°. This advanced variation will also require a second elevation, where you can place the feet. With this it will get harder as there will be more load on the arms to push.


Execution

Step 1: Plance your hands on the first elevation and your feet on the second. Start position is with arms and legs straight, upper body perpendicular to the floor, butt close to the first elvation and hands about shoulder wide apart.

Step 2: Lower yourself down by bending the arms, but keeping the elbows in (close to the body). Go to at least a 90° bend in the arms. Keep the shoulder blades depressed (press them down) and do NOT protracted them (allow them to rotate inwards).

Step 3: Push yourself up until your arms are straight again. Also keep your upper body straight during th whole movement and your hip down.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Elbows in
* Scapula depressed and retracted
* Upper body straight and perpendicular to floor

list.name: Tiger Bend Push Up
list.description:
The Tiger Bend Push Up is a variation which will focus more on the triceps.


Execution

Step 1: Start in a Push Up and lower yourself to the bottom position.

Step 2: Rock your arms back, so that your forearms lay on the ground. Keep the elbows in and a posterior pelvic tilt.

Step 3: Push from your palms to rock yourself back into the bottom position of the Push Up.

Step 4: Push yourself up into the top position of the Push Up.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Posterior pelvic tilt
* Elbows in
* Scapula depressed and protracted

list.name: Russian Dip
list.description:
The Russion Dip also called as Elbow Dip will add a new difficulty and a bit of explosiveness to the regular dip.


Execution

Step 1: Start in a Dip Support position. Shoulder blades depressed and straight arms.

Step 2: Lower yourself down with control into a low Dip position.

Step 3: Rock back and catch yourself with your elbows on the bar. The bar shouldn't be on the bones of your elbows but beside it. Also keep attention to have your shoulders above your elbows and not in front of them. Also keep the shoulder blade depression the whole time.

Step 4: Pull yourself forward into the bottom Dip position.

Step 5: Dip yourself up until your arms a completely straight again.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Elbows in
* Shoulder above elbows
* Scapula depressed

list.name: Ring Dip
list.description:
The Ring Dip will require additional stabilization and will therefore make it harder than a regular Dip on parallel bars.


Execution

Step 1: Start in a Dip Support position. Shoulder blades depressed and straight arms. Keep the rings close to your body during the whole movement.

Step 2: Lower yourself down with control into a low Dip position.

Step 3: Dip yourself up until the arms are completely straight again. You can rotate the rings outwards for an additional stimulus.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Elbows in
* Rings close to the body
* Shoulder blades depressed

list.name: Dip +10Kg
list.description:
The Dip is a fundamental exercise to train your triceps and chest. With an additional weight vest or weight belt you can make the exercise harder.


Execution

Step 1: Adjust the weight vest or weight belt. Start in an upright support hold position. If the bars aren't high enough you can bend your legs. Keep your body straight and your elbows in, in order to target your triceps. If you lean forward and let your elbows go out, more tension will go to your chest and shoulders.

Step 2: Lower yourself until your arms have at least a 90° angle.

Step 3: Push yourself upwards to the initial position without swing.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Shoulder blades depressed
* Elbows in 
* Lower at least to 90° 
* No swing

list.name: Triceps Extension
list.description:
The Triceps Extension targets your triceps muscle. Other muscles e.g. the core are involed for stabilization, but the push should come from the triceps. The difficulty can be adjusted with your hand placement. Put the hands below your chin for a harder variation and in front of your head for an easier variation.


Execution

Step 1: On the ground start in a Plank position with your hands below your head. Keep a hollow body during the movement.

Step 2: Push from the palm of your hands and extend your arms. Keep the rest of the body (especially the shoulders) in the same position. Focus to push from the Triceps.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Hollow Body
* Scapula depressed and protracted
* No back and forth rocking

list.name: Archer Dip
list.description:
Unilateral execices are a great way to increase the difficulty. With the Archer Dip you can put more load to one arm and tackle imbalances. You can perform them on a straight bar, wide parallel bars or rings (which is the best options for the joints).


Execution

Step 1: Start in a top Dip position. Shoulder blades are depressed and sligthly protracted. Arms straight.

Step 2: Lower yourself to the side of one hand until your active arm is bend to at least 90°. The other arm stays straight and helps as little as possible.

Step 3: Push yourself back up into the starting position. Try to keep the elbow in during the dip and your shoulder blades depressed.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Shoulder blades depressed
* Elbow in 
* Lower at least to 90° 
* No swing

list.name: Clap Dip
list.description:
The Cap Dip brings some explosivness to the regular Dip.


Execution

Step 1: Start in a stable support position. Shoulder blades down and arms straight.

Step 2: Lower yourself in a straight line downward until your arms are at least bend to 90°.

Step 3: Reaching the bottom posiiton, don't pause but explosively push yourself up.

Step 4: Quickly clap your hands at the highest position of the jump and move them back to savely catch the landing.

Step 5: Catch the bars and in control lower yourself for the next rep.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Shoulder blades depressed
* Elbows in 
* Lower at least to 90°

list.name: 180 Dip
list.description:
The 180 Dip requires a lot of explosivness.


Execution

Step 1: Start in a stable support position. Shoulder blades down and arms straight.

Step 2: Lower yourself in a straight line downward until your arms are at least bend to 90°.

Step 3: Reaching the bottom posiiton, don't pause but explosively push yourself up and above the bar.

Step 4: Quickly spin to 180° in the air and prepare to savely catch the landing.

Step 5: Catch the bars and in control lower yourself for the next rep.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Shoulder blades depressed
* Elbows in 
* Lower at least to 90°

list.name: Impossible Dip
list.description:
The Impossible Dip is an extrem tough exercise for Pros.


Execution

Step 1: Start in the bottom position of an elbow dip. Elbows on the bar, shoulders behind hands, shoulder blades depressed and chest high.

Step 2: Push yourself up from the force of your triceps, without rocking the shoulders forward. Keep them behind the hands the whole time.

Step 3: Rep is completed with straight arms, hollow body position and shoulders slightly behind the hands.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Shoulder blades depressed
* Elbows in
* Shoulders behind hands
* Hollow Body

list.name: Tiger Bend HSPU
list.description:
The Tiger Bend Handstand Push Up is a harder variation of the regular Handstand Push Up and will require more strength in the triceps.


Execution

Step 1: Start in a Handstand.

Step 2: Lower yourself into the bottom position of a Handstand Push.

Step 3: Instead of pushing up right away, rock back into an Elbow Handstand. This means to land on your foreams and balance your weight, which will need a slightly arched back.

Step 4: Use momentum and a kick from your legs, to rock back into the bottom position of the Handstand Push Up. Back into a hollow body position.

Step 5: Push Up into a Handstand.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Slightly arched back on Elbow Handstand
* Hollow Body on Handstand

list.name: Chair Squat
list.description:
The Chair Squat is a Beginner exercise as step to learn the regular Squat. The support of the Chair will give you a limited range of motion, so the legs build the necessary muscles and mobility to decrease to the height of the chair. The difficulty can be increased by using a smaler chair/elevation.


Execution

Step 1: Sit on the chair/elevation with your legs bend to 90°.

Step 2: Sit up with to a standing position, with your hands in front of. Keep your knees outward, your upper body straight and chest high.

Step 3: Lower yourself until your but touches the chair/elevation. Don't rest here but directly push yourself up into the standing position.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Chest high
* Knees outward
* Straight back

list.name: Squat
list.description:
The Squat is a fundamental exercise for the lower body.

Execution:
The Squat is performed with your feet shoulder wide apart. Tight your abs, keep your back straight and look forward not down (this will help you to keep a straight back). Lower yourself with the weight on your heels. Don't allow your knees to turn inwards. Lower yourself as far you can keep good form. The goal is to have your hip on level with the knees. At the bottom push yourself up through your heels, while keeping good form.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Knee turns out, never in  
* Straight head looking forward not down
* Tight abs
* Push from your heel  
* Straight back

list.name: Curtsy Squat
list.techniqueSummary: * Knee in straight line to foot

list.name: Squat +10Kg
list.description:
The Squat is a fundamental exercise for the lower body. The easiest way to make the exercise harder is to add some weight. This can be a weight vest or barbell with weight plate.

Execution:
The Squat is performed with your feet shoulder wide apart. Tight your abs, keep your back straight and look forward not down (this will help you to keep a straight back). Lower yourself with the weight on your heels. Don't allow your knees to turn inwards. Lower yourself as far you can keep good form. The goal is to have your hip on level with the knees. At the bottom push yourself up through your heels, while keeping good form.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Knee turns out, never in
* Straight head looking forward not down
* Tight abs
* Push from your heel  
* Straight back

list.name: Jump Squat with Tuck
list.description:
The Jump Squat with Tuck exercise will require a lot of explosiveness and jump power, in order to have enough time to tuck the knees and land softly.


Execution

Step 1: Start in a standing position. Feet a bit wider than shoulders apart.

Step 2: With control lower yourself down, keep a straight back.

Step 3: At the lowest position don't stop but use the loaded engery, push from your heels and explode up high.

Step 4: Jump as high as you can and tuck the knee.

Step 5: Softly land and catch the jump into another squat.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Knee turns out, never in
* Straight head looking forward not down
* Tight abs
* Push from your heel  
* Straight back

list.name: One Leg Jump Squat
list.description:
The One Leg Jump Squat requires strong and stable legs. It is a great exercise to build explosiveness and strong legs.


Execution

Step 1: Stand on one foot. Bend the other leg. Keep your balance and straight body.

Step 2: Bend the leg your are standing on and lower yourself to about 45°.

Step 3: From the bottom position explode up and jump as high as you can.

Step 4: Softly absorb the landing by descending into the bottom position of the next rep.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Knee stable
* Soft landing
* Chest high

list.name: Squat +60Kg
list.description:

Execution:

Lower yourself with the weight on your heels. Don't allow your knees to turn inwards. Lower yourself as far you can keep good form. The goal is to have your hip on level with the knees. * Tight abs
* Push from your heel  
* Straight back

list.name: Archer Squat +20Kg
list.description:
The Archer Squat is an unilateral exercise. Some additional weight can be added with a weight vest or by e.g. holding a kettlebell


Execution

Step 1: The Archer Squat starts in a split stand.

Step 2: Shift your weight to one leg and lower yourself to this side. Try to get your hip below your knee, but remain good form (straight back and stable knee).

Step 3: Push from your heel to get back to the starting position.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Knee turns out, never in  
* Straight head looking forward not down
* Tight abs
* Push from your heel  
* Straight back

* Push from your heel  
* Straight back

list.name: Russian Twist
list.description:
The Russian Twist is a good Beginner exercise for the lower abs and obliques.


Execution

Step 1: Sit on the floor, lean back and raise your feet from the ground. Press the heels together and keep your legs stable during the exercise.

Step 2: Twist your upper body to one side and touch the ground with your hands. Go for a full range of motion and really twist your upper body. Keep the legs together and stable.

Step 3: Twist to the other side and touch the ground. One rep requires the twist to both sides.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Heels pressed togerher
* Full twist

list.name: Bicycle
list.description:
The Bicycle is a great exercise for the lower core and obliques.


Execution

Step 1: Start by laying the back, put the hands to the head, raise your head and shoulders from the floor and stretch your legs straight. Engage your core and glute have a flat back against the floor, there should be no arch and space between your lower back and the floor.

Step 2: Bring one leg in, by tucking it to about 90°. The other leg stays straight. The lower you keep it, the harder it is for the lower core. The higher you have the passive leg, the eaiser. At the same time as you bring your leg in, twist your torso, crunch in and bring your opposite elbow to the bend leg. Do NOT bring your chin in, but keep a neutral head position.

Step 3: Stretch the leg again, bring the other one in and twist to the other side. Again touch the knee with the opposite elbow. Touch to both sides counts as one rep.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Head neutral
* Hollow body
* Elbows out
* Shoulders a bit over ground

list.name: Bar Leg Raises
list.description:
Bar Leg Raises are one of the best core exercises if done correctly.


Execution

Step 1: Start in an Active Bar Hang. Keep your arms and legs straight during the whole movement.

Step 2: Raise your legs slowly and in control until they are parallel to the ground. Try to not lean back or to bring your hip up. Use NO swing! Hold the top position shortly.

Step 3: Slowly descent your legs and bring them to a stop a bit in front of you. This will ensure to keep them engaged and to avoid using swing.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Vertical Torso
* Don't lean back extensively
* Together and straight legs
* NO swing

list.name: Jackknife
list.description:
Jackknifes ore V-Sit Ups are a tough and complex core exercise. The most important part to perform this exercise safely is to not arch the back.


Execution

Step 1: Start by laying with the back on the floor. Stretch your arms and legs. Engage your core and glutes to fill the space between your back and the floor. This position is also called a hollow body.

Step 2: Bring your straight arms and legs up until they touch. Keep your back flat and don't arch.

Step 3: Control the down movement and still keep the back flat. Bring the hands and feet back up before they touch the ground.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Keep a hollow body
* Don't arch the back
* Legs straight

list.name: Climber
list.description:
The Mountain Climber is a great exercise to get your blood flow up during a warm up and as cardio exercise.


Execution

Step 1: Start in a top Push Up position. Arms and legs straight. Shoulder blades protracted and depressed. Posterior pelvic tilt. Body in one line. Hip neither too high nor too low.

Step 2: Tuck one leg in. Knee towards chest. Keep the other leg straight and maintain the straight body posture.

Step 3: Bring the leg back and the tuck the other one in.

Step 4: Repeat and do this exercice with a fast speed.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Shoulder blades depressed and protracted
* Posterior pelvic tilt
* Body in straight line

list.name: Knee Side Raise
list.description:
The Knee Side Raise is a beginner exercise which targets the obliques.


Execution

Step 1: Start in Side Plank position, but with your knees on the floor.

Step 2: Bring your hip down to the floor.

Step 3: Raise your hip up as high as you can by engaging your obliques muscle. Keep the stability during the whole movement. Always train both sides.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Keep the stability
* Hip as high as possible

list.name: Side Plank Raise
list.description:
The Side Plank Raise is a beginner exercise, which targets the obliques.


Execution

Step 1: Start in Side Plank position with a straight arm.

Step 2: Bring your hip down to the floor.

Step 3: Raise your hip up as high as you can by engaging your obliques muscle. Keep the stability during the whole movement. Always train both sides.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Keep the stability
* Hip as high as possible
* Straight legs

list.name: Lying Tuck Hold
list.description:
The Lying Tuck Hold is a beginner core exercise, which will target your upper and lower abs at the same time. To make the exercise harder you can switch to the Hollow Body hold.


Execution

Step 1: Lay down and tuck your legs.

Step 2: Engage your abs and raise your shoulders from the floor.

Step 3: Bring your knees to your chest and your nose to your knees. Hold this position.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Tuck knee to chest
* Raise shoulders from floor

list.name: Tuck Reverse Crunch
list.description:
The Tuck Reverse Crunch can be done with or without support. This means you can hold a bar/bench behind your head or do this exercise with your hands on the ground. But in this case, make sure to not use the hands as help and to work only with your abs.


Execution

Step 1: Start with your back on the floor. Legs straight and together. Arms behind your head. Already engage your core, so that there is no arch in your lower back. Ensure that there is no arch during the whole movement.

Step 2: Tuck your legs and bring them to your chest. Hold the top position briefly.

Step 3: Slowly and in control stretch your legs to the starting position.
list.techniqueSummary: * No arch in the back

list.name: Reverse Crunch
list.description:
The Reverse Crunch can be done with or without support. This means you can hold a bar/bench behind your head or do this exercise with your hands on the ground. But in this case, make sure to not use the hands as help and to work only with your abs.


Execution

Step 1: Start with your back on the floor. Legs straight and together. Arms behind your head. Already engage your core, so that there is no arch in your lower back. Ensure that there is no arch during the whole movement.

Step 2: Raise your legs and bring them to your chest. When they are in a 90° angle, push your hip up but make sure that the feet are still in front of your head. Hold the top position briefly.

Step 3: Slowly and in control come back and stretch your legs to the starting position.
list.techniqueSummary:
* No arch in the back
* Legs stay in front of the head
* Legs straight

list.name: Cross Climber
list.description:
The Cross Climber is a variation of the regular Mountain Climber exercise. With the extra twist it will also engage the oblique muscles.


Execution

Step 1: Start in the top position of a Push Up. Keep a posterior pelvic tilt, which means to engage your glutes and to rotate your hip inwards.

Step 2: Bring one knee to the elbow of the opposite side. Try to bring it in as far as possible and even touch the elbow if possible. Control the movement with your core and don't use momentum as help.

Step 3: Bring your leg back to the Push Up position and repeat with the other side.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Posterior Pelvic Tilt
* Keep hip stable and on same level
* Shoulders stay directly over hands
* Controlled movement

list.name: Plank Walk
list.description:
The Plank Walk is a great but much harder variation of the Plank exercise. By walking backwards the lever increases and needs much more stabilization in the core.


Execution

Step 1: Start in a Plank position. Keep a posterior pelvic tilt, which means to engage your glutes and to rotate your hip inwards. Shoulder blades are slightly protracted.

Step 2: With slow and small steps walk backwards to increase the lever. Keep your hip on the same level and your core engaged. Don't raise your glutes and keep the scapula protraction. Stop the backwards walk, before your form breaks.

Step 3: Slowly walk forward and return to the Plank position.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Posterior Pelvic Tilt
* Keep hip stable and on same level
* Protract scapula
* Controlled movement

list.name: Hollow Body Rocks
list.description:
The Hollow Body Rock adds some dynamic to the Hollow Body Hold. The difficulty increase with the back and forth rocking.


Execution

Step 1: Start in a Hollow Body Hold.

Step 2: Start to rock back and forth as smooth as possible. Therefore keep the form of the Hollow Body and don't arch the back during the movement.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Posterior pelvic tilt
* Don't arch the back

list.name: Adv. Plank Walk
list.description:
The Plank Walk is a great but much harder variation of the Plank exercise. By walking backwards the lever increases and needs much more stabilization in the core. In this advanced version try to walk backwards until your arms are straight, but only while maintaining proper form.


Execution

Step 1: Start in a Plank position but with your arms straight. Keep a posterior pelvic tilt, which means to engage your glutes and to rotate your hip inwards. Shoulder blades are slightly protracted.

Step 2: With slow and small steps walk backwards to increase the lever. Keep your hip on the same level and your core engaged. Don't raise your glutes and keep the scapula protraction. Stop the backwards walk, before your form breaks.

Step 3: Slowly walk forward and return to the Plank position.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Posterior Pelvic Tilt
* Keep hip stable and on same level
* Protract scapula
* Controlled movement

list.name: Tuck Dragon Flag
list.description:
The Tuck Dragon Flag is a progression to the Dragon Flag. The necessary core strength is significantly reduced, but it is a good preparation to understand the movement pattern and to test the necessary strength in the back and arms.


Execution

Step 1: Find a spot where you can grab something stable behind your head. This could be a bench, a couch or a bed. Keep your hands close to your head and your elbows in. The exercise starts in the top position and with your legs tucked in.

Step 2: Slowly lower yourself towards the ground, while keeping the core engaged and your legs tucked. Keep your back straight and don't let it arch.

Step 3: Just before touching the ground, pull yourself back up.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Hands close to head
* Elbows tucked in
* Hollow Body
* Posterior Pelvic Tilt
* Straight back without arching

list.name: Half Dragon Flag
list.description:
The Half Dragon Flag is a progression to the Dragon Flag. The difficulty is decreased by tucking the legs back.


Execution

Step 1: Find a spot where you can grab something stable behind your head. This could be a bench, a couch or a bed. Keep your hands close to your head and your elbows in. The exercise starts in the top position and with your legs tucked back.

Step 2: Slowly lower yourself towards the ground, while keeping the core engaged and your leg positioning. Keep your back straight and don't let it arch.

Step 3: Just before touching the ground, pull yourself back up.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Hands close to head
* Elbows tucked in
* Hollow Body
* Posterior Pelvic Tilt
* Straight back without arching

list.name: One Leg Dragon Flag
list.description:
The One Leg Dragon Flag is a progression to the Dragon Flag. The difficulty is decreased by tucking one leg in, which will counterbalance the lever. Keep attention on how much you tuck your leg in and progress over time to only a 90° tuck.


Execution

Step 1: Find a spot where you can grab something stable behind your head. This could be a bench, a couch or a bed. Keep your hands close to your head and your elbows in. The exercise starts in the top position and with on leg tucked in and the other one straight.

Step 2: Slowly lower yourself towards the ground, while keeping the core engaged and your leg positioning. Keep your back straight and don't let it arch.

Step 3: Just before touching the ground, pull yourself back up.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Hands close to head
* Elbows tucked in
* Hollow Body
* Posterior Pelvic Tilt
* Straight back without arching

list.name: Straddle Dragon Flag
list.description:
The Straddle Dragon Flag is a progression to the Dragon Flag. The difficulty is sligthly decreased by spreading the legs. The more you can spread them, the easier it gets. Don't bend in your hips.


Execution

Step 1: Find a spot where you can grab something stable behind your head. This could be a bench, a couch or a bed. Keep your hands close to your head and your elbows in. The exercise starts in the top position with your legs straight but spread.

Step 2: Slowly lower yourself towards the ground, while keeping the core engaged and your leg positioning. Keep your back straight and don't let it arch.

Step 3: Just before touching the ground, pull yourself back up.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Hands close to head
* Elbows tucked in
* Hollow Body
* Posterior Pelvic Tilt
* Straight back without arching

list.name: Negative Dragon Flag
list.description:
The Negative Dragon Flag is an eccentric movement which will allow you to work over 100% of your strength and in the correct/final body positioning.


Execution

Step 1: u can spread them, the easier it gets. Don't bend in your hips.


Execution

Step 1: Find a spot where you can grab something stable behind your head. This could be a bench, a couch or a bed. Keep your hands close to your head and your elbows in. The exercise starts in the top position with your legs straight.

Step 2: Slowly lower yourself towards the ground, while keeping the core engaged and your leg positioning. Keep your back straight and don't let it arch.

Step 3: Lower yourself completly and swing back up with tucked legs for the next rep.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Hands close to head
* Elbows tucked in
* Hollow Body
* Posterior Pelvic Tilt
* Straight back without arching

list.name: Cobra Pose
list.description:
The Cobra Stretch or Cobra Pose can be used as stretching exercises after working on your core.


Execution

Step 1: Lay down with your belly on the floor. Hands are in front of the shoulders.

Step 2: Slowly press through your hands and lift your chest. Keep your feet and hip on the ground. Egange the glutes a bit to focus the stretch on the abs.

Step 3: Hold the top position. Push your shoulder blades down and bak. Arch only as possible without pain.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Hips and feet stay on ground
* Lift Chest up
* Engage Glutes

list.name: Bodyweight Curl
list.description:
The Bodyweight Curl is best done with rings, as you can adjust the height to your needs and because the rings will as you pull up. As alternative you can do this exercise also on a low bar or parallel bar.


Execution

Step 1: Start with your feet in front the bar/rings. Use a strong supinated grip. Stretch your arms and lean back. Keep your body tight.

Step 2: In contrast to the body row, you have to keep your elbows always in front of you. So only bend in your forearms and pull the rings/bar towards your head.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Elbows in front of you
* Supinated grip
* Core and glutes tight

list.name: Reverse Bodyweight Curl
list.description:
The Reverse Bodyweight Curl is best done with rings, as you can adjust the height to your needs and because the rings will move with as you lean forward. As alternative you can do this exercise also on a low bar or parallel bar. The rings or bar should be ideally about hip level.


Execution

Step 1: Place the feet a few steps behind the rings. Firmly (false) grip the rings and hold them besides your abs. Your body should be in a hollow position during this movement.

Step 2: Slowly lean forwars and extend your arms. Don't let the rings to spread too wide apart and keep your elbows in.

Step 3: Curl yourself up by pulling the rings towards your armpit, until they touch your back. Keep attention to only pull with your arms and not the shoulder or by changing your body position. You should keep the hollow body position.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Hollow Body
* Elbows in
* Keep scapula retracted
* Only bend forearms

list.name: Adv. Triceps Extension
list.description:
The Advanced Triceps Extension targets your triceps muscle. This advanced variation allows for more range of motion. Other muscles e.g. the core are involed for stabilization, but the push should come from the triceps. To make the exercise easier, just choose a bar/ring setup, which is more vertical. The lower the bar/rings are to the ground, the harder.


Execution

Step 1: Grip the bar/rings and start with your arms stretched, like in a top Push Up position but with your arms in front of you. Keep a hollow body during the movement.

Step 2: Lower yourself only by bending the arms, until your head is below the bar / between the rings.

Step 3: Push from your hands and extend your arms. Keep the rest of the body (especially the shoulders) in the same position. Focus to push from the Triceps and not to involve other big muscles as help.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Hollow Body
* Scapula depressed and protracted
* Push only through triceps

list.name: Ring Row
list.description:
The Body Row exercise targets primarily your back muscles and biceps. Performing it on rings will give you multiple benefits. 1. You can adjust the difficulty as needed. 2. The instability of the rings will add an extra challenge.


Execution:

Step 1. Set up the rings to about hip level, while standing.

Step 2: Adjust the difficulty through the foot placement and body positioning. Grab the rings and straighten the arms. Walk forward and bring your body into a more horizontal position to make it harder. Walk back and bring your body into a more standing position to make it easier.

Step 3: Lean back, keep the arms and body straight. Bring your chest up and engage your core.

Pull your shoulder blades back and down as you bend your elbows and pull your chest towards the rings.

Pause when your chest is close to the rings, then slowly lower yourself back down to the starting position with control.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Straight body
* Scapula retracted and depressed

list.name: Ring Push Up
list.description:
The Push Up is a basic exercise to build chest and triceps strength. Performing it on rings will give you multiple benefits.  1. You can adjust the difficulty as needed. 2. The instability of the rings will add an extra challenge.


Execution:

Setup the rings close to the ground. Grab the rings and start in a plank position with your arms a bit more than shoulder wide apart. Your hands should be in line with your shoulders. Press your shoulders down (proctracted) and back (depressed). Turn your pelves in and keep a good tension in your whole body. Slowly lower yourself down and don't allow your elbows to turn widely outwards. When your chest is between the rings, feel how your shoulder blades squeeze together and push yourself back up.
Variations: There are a lot of variations of the Push Up. For example you can make it easier by walking forward. This will bring your straight body into a more diagonal position, which will reduce the load. To make it harder, bring the rings as close as possible to the floor.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Keep elbows in
* Scapula protracted
* Till pelves backward
* Tight body
* Full range of motion (ROM)
* Don't arch back

list.name: Ring Curl
list.description:
With the Ring Curl you can adjust the difficulty to your needs. Just setup the rings higher to make it easier, or lower to make it harder, as you will have more weight on the rings, in a more horizontal position. As alternative you can do this exercise also on a low bar or Dip Bar.


Execution

Step 1: Start with your feet in front the bar/rings. Use a strong supinated grip. Stretch your arms and lean back. Keep your body tight.

Step 2: In contrast to the body row, you have to keep your elbows always in front of you. So only bend in your forearms and pull the rings/bar towards your head.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Elbows in front of you
* Supinated grip
* Core and glutes tight

list.name: Ring Triceps Extension
list.description:
The Triceps Extension targets your triceps muscle. Other muscles e.g. the core are involed for stabilization, but the push should come from the triceps. The difficulty can be adjusted by the height of the rings. The higher the rings are from the ground, the easier.


Execution

Step 1: Setup the rings, grab them and start with your straight arms in front of your head. Keep a hollow body during the movement.

Step 2: Slowly bend your arms and bring the rings towards your head.

Step 3: Push by extendind your arms. Keep the rest of the body (especially the shoulders) in the same position. Focus to push from the Triceps.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Hollow Body
* Scapula depressed and protracted

list.name: Ring Support Hold
list.description: Holding a support position in the rings requires some practice in the beginning. As the rings are unstable, you need to pull them towards your body and keep the necessary body tension. Keep your arms straight and your shoulder blades depressed.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Straight Arms
* Hollow Body
* Scapula depressed

list.name: Ring Pull Up
list.description:
The Pull Up is a basic calisthenic exercise, which is a precondition for more advanced skills.

Execution:
The exercise starts in a dead hang. The movement is initialized through depressing and retracting the scapula. Next you pull yourself up by engaging your biceps and back muscles. Pull till your chin surpasses the your hands (even better till your chest touches the rings). Descend slowly and controlled into a dead hang position. It is important to always perform the full range of motion.

Variations:
The Chin-Up is done with an underhand grip in contrast to the overhand grip of the Pull-Up. With the underhand grip your elbows naturally bend more inwards, by what your biceps will be targeted to a greater extent. Instead of rings you can perform the Pull-Up also on a bar.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Scapula depressed and retracted
* No swing 
* Full range of motion (ROM)

list.name: Ring Muscle Up
list.description:
The Muscle-Up can be divided into 3 phases:

         1. Pull
         2. Transition
         3. Push

Start in a dead hang and grab the rings with a false grip. Pull the rings below your armpits. It is easier if you keep them close to your body. The probably toughest part is the transition. To succeed you'll need to throw your shoulders forward and rotate your hands on top of the rings. The last step is a Ring Dip.

The Muscle-up needs almost all muscles in your upper body. It needs explosivness and a good coordination of the muscles. The Muscle-up can also be performed on a bar.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Keep rings close to body 
* Keep elbows in 
* Hollow Body

list.name: Bulgarian Ring Dip
list.description:
The Bulgarian Ring Dip is a variation of the regular Ring Dip, with the goal to make the exercise a bit more challenging.


Execution

Step 1: Start in a Support position. Shoulder blades depressed and straight arms. Instead of keeping the rings close to the body the whole time, this will change at the bottom position for this exercise variation.

Step 2: Lower yourself down with control into a low Dip position and simultaneously spread your the rings to the side to upper arm widht. In the end position you will have a nice 90°/90° position of the arms.

Step 3: Push yourself up until the arms are completely straight again. You can rotate the rings outwards for an additional stimulus.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Elbows in
* Rings close to the body
* Shoulder blades depressed

list.name: Ring Archer Row
list.description:
The Archer Row is a great way to increase the difficulty of a Body Row, by making it a unilateral exercise.


Execution

Step 1: Setup the rings about hip height or higher. Grab the rings and keep your body and legs in a straight line. The rings should be in front of your lower chest.

Step 2: Lean back and straighten your arms. Keep your chest high and your shoulder blades neutral or slightly retracted.

Step 3: Pull your chest towards one ring side by bending your active arm and squeeze your back muscles (retract your shoulder blades). Try to keep your passive arm as straight as possible. Keep your shoulder blades depressed and don't allow them to come up and round your back. Keep your elbows in and pull them back.

Step 4: Hold the top position briefly and always keep your body in a straight line.

Step 5: Train both sides
list.techniqueSummary:
* Straight body
* Scapula depressed and retracted

list.name: Ring Archer Push Up
list.description:
The Archer Push Up is an intermediate exercise to build chest and triceps strength.

Execution:
Setup the rings and start in a plank position. Press your shoulders down (proctracted) and back (depressed). Turn your pelves in and keep a good tension in your whole body. Slowly lower yourself down to one side by bending one arm and keeping the other one straight (the more you bend the assisting arm, the easier the exercise gets). Don't allow the elbow of your bending arm to turn widely outwards. Before your chest is about ring level. Feel how your shoulder blades squeeze together and push yourself back up.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Assisting arm straight
* Keep elbows in
* Scapula protracted
* Till pelves backward
* Tight body
* Full range of motion (ROM)
* Don't arch back

list.name: Ring Skin the Cat
list.description:
Execution:
Skin the cat starts in a dead hang position. Pull your legs up and continue rotating them past your elbows, while tucking them towards your chest. Continue the rotation until your toes point to the ground. Try to really extend yourself and carefully stretch your shoulders. In this bottom position your legs should be straight and your arms fully extended. Start the rewind through pulling with your shoulder and bringing your legs to your chest. 
This exercise is great to build shoulder strength and mobility.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Straight arms 
* Scapula protracted 
Keep your core tight

list.name: Ring Pistol Squat
list.description:
Execution:
The Ring Pistol Squat should accustom you to a full range of motion. Lack in strength and balance can be settled through the assistance of pulling with your arms. You can use different objects for assistance (doorway, table, pole, TRX, rings). Try to perform the same movement like with a full Pistol Squat aside the assistance.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Knee turns out, never in  
* Straight head looking forward not down
* Tight abs
* Push from your heel  
* Straight back

list.name: Bulgarian Ring Squat
list.description:
The Bulgarian Squat should help you to build one leg strength and a better balance.

Execution:
It is performed with one leg resting in a ring and the other leg in front of you, where the majority of your weight should be shifted. Lower yourself controlled into a lunge and if needed help balance with your rear leg. Keep attention that your knee doesn't surpass your toe. At the bottom push through your heel to get up again. Keep a straight back.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Knee turns out, never in
* Knees behind toes
* Straight head looking forward not down
* Tight abs
* Push from your heel  
* Straight back

list.name: Ring Glute Bridge
list.description:
Execution:
Lay with your back down on the floor. Put your feet on the rings and tuck your legs to 90°. Bring the rings to your glutes and raise your hip as high as you can. Pause for 1/2 sec at the top and then repeat.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Extend hip as high as possible
* Tight core

list.name: Ring Rollout
list.description:
The Ring Rollout is an exercise that targets the abs.


Execution:

Step 1: Setup the rings below hip level. Grab the rings and start in a top Push Up position

Step 2: Tighten your core muscles and slowly bring the rings forward. Keep your back straight and your abs engaged. As you roll forward, your arms should extend in front of you.

Step 3: Continue rolling forward until your body is parallel to the floor, or until you feel your form break.

Step 4: Pause for a second or two, then slowly roll back to the starting position, keeping your core engaged the entire time. Repeat the exercise for the desired number of repetitions.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Posterior Pelvic Tilt
* Straight arms
* Protract scapula
* Controlled movement

list.name: Toes to Rings
list.description:
Execution:
Start in a dead hang position. Bring your feet up to touch the barrings. Keep your toes together and your legs straight
list.techniqueSummary:
* Straight arms
* Straight legs
* Tight core

list.name: Ring Leg Raises
list.description:
Ring Leg Raises are one of the best core exercises if done correctly.


Execution

Step 1: Start in an Active Hang on rings. Keep your arms and legs straight during the whole movement.

Step 2: Raise your legs slowly and in control until they are parallel to the ground. Try to not lean back or to bring your hip up. Use NO swing! Hold the top position shortly.

Step 3: Slowly descent your legs and bring them to a stop a bit in front of you. This will ensure to keep them engaged and to avoid using swing.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Vertical Torso
* Don't lean back extensively
* Together and straight legs
* NO swing

list.name: Ring Knee Raises
list.description:
Knee Raises are a basic exercise to train your abs. You will need your hip flexors, lower abs and in the end your upper abs

Execution:
Start in a dead hang and don't arch your back, try to have a hollow body position. Slowly pull your knees and hip up towards your chest, by engaging your abs. Don't just swing the legs up, but be in control of the movement. At the top your knees should be at least over hip level. Pause there briefly and slowly lower your legs. Keep a hollow body and don't arch your back.
Variations: You can also perform Knee Raises on a Pull Up Bar, Parallel Bars or even between chairs. You can increase the difficulty by straighting your legs.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Don't swing 
* Engage Abs 
* Knees above hip level 
* Brief pause on top 
* Slow and controlled move

list.name: Row with Band
list.description:
The Band Row is an exercise which can be done almost everywhere and is good for beginners. Before doing this exercise you must ensure that every object (door, pole, towel, band, ...) you are using is stable enough


Execution

Step 1: Find a stable door handle or pole. Knot the towel/band across it. Ensure the stability.

Step 2: Grab the towel/band with straight arms. Go into a seated position and lean back a bit, but keep your back straight.

Step 3: Press your shoulder blades down and retract them during the movement. Bring the towel/band towards your stomach and pull your elbows close and back. Hold the end position a bit. 

Step 4: With control straighten your arms again into the starting position.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Straight back
* Shoulder blades down and back
* Elbows close and back

list.name: Pulldown with Band
list.description:
Performing a lat pulldown exercise with a resistance band is a great alternative if you don't have access to a pulldown machine or a cable station.


Execution:

Step 1: Attach the band to a secure anchor point above your head (e.g. pull-up bar, a beam, ...).

Step 2: Grab the band and kneel or step back to create tension.

Step 3: Initiate the movement by pulling your shoulder blades down and back. Keeping your arms straight, pull the band down towards your chest/belly by engaging your lats. 

Step 4: Pause briefly at the bottom and then repeat.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Straight arms and back
* Shoulder blades down and back
* No excessive momentum

list.name: Bicep Curl with Band
list.description: todo
list.techniqueSummary:
* Engage Biceps
* Shoulder blades down
* Elbows close and back
* No excessive momentum

list.name: Push Up with Band
list.description:
The Push Up is a basic exercise to build chest and triceps strength.

Execution:
Grab the band with one hand and wrap it around your back to grab it with the other hand. Start in a plank position with your arms a bit more than shoulder wide apart. Your hands should be in line with your shoulders. Press your shoulders down (proctracted) and back (depressed). Turn your pelves in and keep a good tension in your whole body. Slowly lower yourself down and don't allow your elbows to turn widely outwards. Before your chest almost touches the ground, feel how your shoulder blades squeeze together and push yourself back up.
Variations: There are a lot of variations of the Push Up. For example you can make them easier by doing it inclined against a bench. You can make it more dynamic with Clapping Push Ups or harder witch Archer Push Ups or even One Arm Push Ups.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Keep elbows in
* Scapula protracted
* Till pelves backward
* Tight body
* Full range of motion (ROM)
* Don't arch back

list.name: Shoulder Press with Band
list.description:
Execution:

Step 1: Stand on the band with your feet shoulder-width apart. Grab the band and bring your hands up to shoulder level.

Step 2: Engage your core and press the band straight up overhead by extending your arms fully and keeping the elbows in.

Step 3: Slowly lower the band back to the starting position by bending your elbows and bringing your hands back down to shoulder level.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Controlled movement
* No arched back
* Elbows close and back

list.name: Triceps Extension with Band
list.description:
Execution:

Step 1: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a resistance band with both hands, palms facing each other.

Step 2: Fully extend your arms overhead and keep elbows close to your head.

Step 3: Pause briefly before lowering the band behind your head, to start a new rep.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Straight back
* Keep elbows in

list.name: Squat with Band
list.description:
The Squat is a fundamental exercise for the lower body.

Execution:

Step 1: Step on the band with your feet at least shoulder wide apart. Bring the band on top of your shoulders. You can hold it like a barbell if you like.

Step 2: Tight your abs, keep your back straight and look forward not down (this will help you to keep a straight back). Lower yourself with the weight on your heels. Don't allow your knees to turn inwards. Lower to about 90° or below, if your mobility allows it.

Step 3: At the bottom push yourself up through your heels, while keeping good form.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Knee turns out, never in  
* Straight head looking forward not down
* Tight abs
* Push from your heel  
* Straight back

list.name: Deadlift with Band
list.description:
Execution:

Step 1: Step on the band with both feet, positioning it in the middle. Grab the ends of the band with your hands, shoulder-width apart and palms facing your body. Maintain a straight back, neutral spine, and engaged core. Bend your knees slightly and push your hips back while keeping tension on the band.

Step 2: Start the lift by driving through your heels, extend your hips forward, and straighten your knees to stand up.

Step 3: Lower the band by reversing the movement, pushing your hips back and bending your knees.
list.techniqueSummary:
* Straight back
* Neutral head position
* Engage Core
* Shoulders slightly down and back
